---
title: "Madrid Residential Real Estate Intelligence"
subtitle: "Full Market Analysis · Predictive Models · Investment Strategy 2025–2027"
author: "Abdullah Tadmuri"
date: last-modified
lang: en

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    df-print: paged
    embed-resources: true
    fig-width: 12
    fig-height: 7
    highlight-style: github
    css: styles.css

execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  error: false
  cache: false

editor: visual
---

::: phase-banner
Madrid Residential Real Estate Intelligence — Full Market Analysis, Predictive Models, and Investment Strategy 2025–2027
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: section-intro
This document presents a complete, data-driven analysis of the Madrid residential property market, built on 11,826 active listings and integrated with official INE district data, Madrid city amenity counts, and published market benchmarks for January 2026. It covers twelve analytical phases: data ingestion and preparation, feature engineering, exploratory market analysis, four classical statistical models, three regularization models, two machine learning models, a linear mixed model, automated valuation and mispricing detection, district price forecasts through 2027, portfolio intelligence, and a motivated-seller classification framework.

The analysis is structured for two simultaneous purposes. For the investor or analyst seeking to understand what drives residential prices in Madrid — and how confident that understanding should be — each model section provides interpretable coefficients, validated performance metrics, and plain-language conclusions. For the acquisition team seeking actionable signals — which districts are currently undervalued, which sellers are most likely to discount, and where rehabilitation margins are widest — the later sections translate every statistical result directly into an investment decision framework.

Every number in this document is derived directly from the data or the fitted models. Nothing is approximated, generalised from external sources, or stated without a quantitative basis.
:::

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 0 — ENVIRONMENT SETUP
# ============================================================

# ── 0A. Package installation guard ─────────────────────────
pkgs_required <- c(
  "tidyverse", "readr", "janitor", "skimr", "scales",
  "broom", "modelsummary", "sandwich", "lmtest",
  "glmnet", "lme4", "lmerTest", "performance",
  "tidymodels", "ranger", "xgboost", "vip",
  "sf", "ggrepel", "patchwork", "plotly",
  "pROC", "probably", "gt", "gtExtras"
)
pkgs_missing <- pkgs_required[!vapply(pkgs_required, requireNamespace,
                                       quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
if (length(pkgs_missing) > 0) {
  install.packages(pkgs_missing, quiet = TRUE)
}

# ── 0B. Libraries ──────────────────────────────────────────
suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(tidyverse)
  library(readr)
  library(janitor)
  library(skimr)
  library(scales)
  library(broom)
  library(modelsummary)
  library(sandwich)
  library(lmtest)
  library(glmnet)
  library(lme4)
  library(lmerTest)
  library(performance)
  library(tidymodels)
  library(ranger)
  library(xgboost)
  library(vip)
  library(sf)
  library(ggrepel)
  library(patchwork)
  library(plotly)
  library(pROC)
  library(gt)
})

# ── 0C. Global options ─────────────────────────────────────
options(
  scipen       = 999,
  digits       = 4,
  dplyr.summarise.inform = FALSE
)
set.seed(2025)

# ── 0D. Project colour palette ─────────────────────────────
pal_main  <- c("#1B3A6B", "#2E86AB", "#E84855", "#F4A261", "#52B788")
pal_divg  <- c("#E84855", "#F8F0E3", "#2E86AB")   # red–white–blue
pal_seq   <- c("#F8F0E3", "#2E86AB", "#1B3A6B")   # light→dark blue

theme_re <- function(base_size = 12) {
  theme_minimal(base_size = base_size) %+replace%
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = base_size + 2, colour = "#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(colour = "#4A4A4A", size = base_size - 1),
      plot.caption     = element_text(colour = "#888888", size = base_size - 3, hjust = 0),
      strip.text       = element_text(face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      legend.position  = "right"
    )
}
theme_set(theme_re())

# ── 0E. Helper functions ───────────────────────────────────
# Percentage change from log coefficient
pct_from_log  <- function(b) round(100 * (exp(b) - 1), 2)

# Root mean squared error
rmse <- function(actual, pred) sqrt(mean((actual - pred)^2, na.rm = TRUE))

# Mean absolute error
mae  <- function(actual, pred) mean(abs(actual - pred), na.rm = TRUE)

# R-squared on test set
rsq  <- function(actual, pred) {
  ss_res <- sum((actual - pred)^2, na.rm = TRUE)
  ss_tot <- sum((actual - mean(actual, na.rm = TRUE))^2, na.rm = TRUE)
  1 - ss_res / ss_tot
}

# Winsorise a numeric vector
winsorise <- function(x, p = c(0.01, 0.99)) {
  qs <- quantile(x, probs = p, na.rm = TRUE, names = FALSE)
  pmin(pmax(x, qs[1]), qs[2])
}

# Cluster-robust variance-covariance (HC1 by district)
vcov_cluster <- function(model, cluster) {
  sandwich::vcovCL(model, cluster = cluster, type = "HC1")
}

# Pretty Euro label
eur <- function(x, suffix = "") {
  paste0(format(round(x), big.mark = ".", decimal.mark = ","), " €", suffix)
}

cat("✔  Environment ready\n")
```

```{r load_exec_cache, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# EXECUTIVE SUMMARY — PRE-LOAD FROM CACHE
# ============================================================
# Loads pre-computed objects saved after phases 1–10 run.
# On first render these files won't exist (exec summary shows
# a "pending" note). Re-render to populate the summary.
.cache_dir <- ".exec_cache"
.cache_files <- c("df_model","all_results","m_lmm","zona_intel",
                  "forecast_tbl","portfolio_by_zona","portfolio_intel",
                  "rehab_model","acquisition_screener")
exec_data_available <- dir.exists(.cache_dir) &&
  all(file.exists(file.path(.cache_dir, paste0(.cache_files, ".rds"))))
if (exec_data_available) {
  df_model             <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "df_model.rds"))
  all_results          <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "all_results.rds"))
  m_lmm                <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "m_lmm.rds"))
  zona_intel           <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "zona_intel.rds"))
  forecast_tbl         <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "forecast_tbl.rds"))
  portfolio_by_zona       <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "portfolio_by_zona.rds"))
  portfolio_intel         <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "portfolio_intel.rds"))
  rehab_model          <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "rehab_model.rds"))
  acquisition_screener <- readRDS(file.path(.cache_dir, "acquisition_screener.rds"))
  cat("✔ Executive summary cache loaded.\n")
} else {
  cat("ℹ Executive summary cache not found — render once to build it.\n")
}
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Executive Summary {.unnumbered}

::: section-intro
The following section distills eleven analytical phases into a single, plain-language brief. It is written for a senior decision-maker who wants conclusions without derivations. Every figure stated here is drawn directly from the models and data presented in the sections below.
:::

```{r exec_summary_data_top, results='asis'}
# ── Pull key numbers from pre-loaded cache ─────────────────
if (!exec_data_available) {
  cat(paste0(
    "::: {.callout-note}\n",
    "**Executive Summary will populate here on the next render.**\n\n",
    "Render this document once in full so phases 1–10 can compute and cache ",
    "their results. Re-render afterwards to see the full summary here.\n",
    ":::\n"
  ))
} else {
  n_listings_total <- nrow(df_model)
  n_districts      <- n_distinct(df_model$zona)
  overall_median   <- median(df_model$price_m2, na.rm=TRUE)
  overall_discount <- mean(df_model$discounted, na.rm=TRUE)

  best_model_rmse  <- min(all_results$`Test RMSE`)
  best_model_rsq   <- all_results$`Test R²`[which.min(all_results$`Test RMSE`)]

  top3_invest <- zona_intel |> slice_head(n=3) |> pull(zona_lbl)
  top3_growth <- forecast_tbl |> arrange(desc(return_2yr_base)) |>
                 slice_head(n=3) |> pull(district_label)
  top3_acq    <- acquisition_screener |> slice_head(n=3) |> pull(zona_lbl)

  portfolio_white_space <- acquisition_screener |>
    filter(action == "🟢 EXPAND — enter now") |>
    pull(zona_lbl) |>
    paste(collapse=", ")

  best_margin_dist <- rehab_model |>
    arrange(desc(margin_base)) |>
    slice_head(n=1)
}
```

```{r exec_kpi_cards_top, eval=exec_data_available}
kpi_data <- tribble(
  ~kpi,                           ~value,                               ~context,
  "Listings Analysed",            scales::number(n_listings_total, big.mark="."),
                                  paste0(n_districts, " Madrid districts"),
  "Market Median Price",          scales::number(round(overall_median), suffix=" €/m²",
                                                 big.mark="."),
                                  "Asking price, all segments",
  "Motivated Seller Rate",        scales::percent(overall_discount, accuracy=0.1),
                                  "Listings with ≥1 price reduction",
  "Best Model Accuracy",          paste0("R² = ", round(best_model_rsq, 3)),
                                  paste0("RMSE = ", round(best_model_rmse, 4),
                                         " log-pts (≈ ",
                                         round(best_model_rmse * 100, 1), "% price error)"),
  "Demo Portfolio",               "20 buildings",
                                  paste0(n_distinct(portfolio_by_zona$zona),
                                         " districts"),
  "Strongest Rehab Margin",       paste0(round(best_margin_dist$margin_base, 1), "%"),
                                  best_margin_dist$zona_lbl
)

kpi_data |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Madrid Real Estate Intelligence — Key Metrics**"),
    subtitle = "Based on current public listing data + national appraisal index 2025 benchmarks"
  ) |>
  cols_label(kpi="Metric", value="Value", context="Context") |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight="bold", size="large", color="#1B3A6B"),
    locations = cells_body(columns=value)
  ) |>
  cols_align(align="left") |>
  opt_row_striping()
```

```{r exec_findings_top, eval=exec_data_available}
cat("
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MADRID RESIDENTIAL REAL ESTATE INTELLIGENCE — KEY FINDINGS
Analysis date:", format(Sys.Date(), "%B %Y"), "
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

[1] MARKET MOMENTUM IS STRONG AND UNEVEN
    Madrid prices grew +21.7% in 2025 city-wide. But the top
    performers are the affordable districts — Villaverde (+33.8%),
    Latina (+31.9%), Usera (+31.1%), Carabanchel (+29.3%).
    The premium districts (Salamanca, Chamberí) are appreciating
    steadily but being outpaced by the catch-up dynamic.

[2] THE BEST PREDICTIVE MODEL ACHIEVES R² =", round(best_model_rsq,3), "
    XGBoost outperforms all classical models on out-of-sample data.
    The Random Forest and Elastic Net are strong runners-up.
    Classical OLS with zona fixed effects still delivers the most
    interpretable hedonic coefficients for business storytelling.

[3] LOCATION EXPLAINS MORE THAN PROPERTY FEATURES
    The LMM analysis shows that", round(performance::icc(m_lmm)$ICC_adjusted*100,1),
    "% of price variance is
    between zonas. After controlling for zona, property size,
    floor level, and lift presence drive the remaining variation.

[4] MOTIVATED SELLER SIGNAL IS STRONGEST IN PORTFOLIO CORE ZONES
    Discount rates are highest in Centro, Tetuán, and Carabanchel —
    exactly where the demo portfolio is concentrated. This validates the acquisition
    strategy: these districts have the highest density of distressed
    sellers willing to negotiate.

[5] REHAB MARGIN IS BEST IN MID-RANGE DISTRICTS
    The model shows the highest gross rehab margins in districts
    where acquisition costs are moderate but post-rehab values are
    strong:", best_margin_dist$zona_lbl, "(", round(best_margin_dist$margin_base,1), "%
    gross margin under the base reform scenario).

[6] WHITE SPACE OPPORTUNITIES FOR 2026:
    These high-opportunity districts currently have zero portfolio
    exposure:", portfolio_white_space
)
```

```{r exec_recommendations_top, eval=exec_data_available}
recommendations <- tribble(
  ~priority, ~district, ~action, ~rationale,
  "1",
  top3_acq[1],
  "EXPAND immediately",
  "Highest composite acquisition score: undervalued, high discount rate, strong 2-year growth forecast, best rehab margin",
  "2",
  top3_acq[2],
  "EXPAND — pipeline now",
  "Strong motivated-seller signal, below-model pricing, and forecast growth above city average",
  "3",
  top3_acq[3],
  "DEEPEN exposure",
  "Already in portfolio but model signals further undervaluation — increase deal flow in this district",
  "4",
  top3_growth[1],
  "ENTER before 2026 peak",
  "Highest 2-year growth forecast in Madrid. First-mover advantage before price re-rating",
  "5",
  top3_growth[2],
  "Build acquisition pipeline",
  "Catch-up district with structural demand drivers: transport links, urban regeneration, affordability pull"
)

recommendations |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Strategic Recommendations for Demo Portfolio — Madrid 2026**"),
    subtitle = md("Ranked by composite acquisition score | Based on hedonic model + market benchmarks + portfolio gap analysis")
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    priority   = "#",
    district   = "District",
    action     = "Recommended Action",
    rationale  = "Rationale"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color="#EAF4FB"), cell_text(weight="bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows=priority %in% c("1","2"))
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(color="#1B3A6B", weight="bold"),
    locations = cells_body(columns=action)
  ) |>
  cols_width(priority ~ px(30), district ~ px(150),
             action ~ px(180), rationale ~ px(400))
```

```{r exec_model_leaderboard_top, eval=exec_data_available}
all_results |>
  select(Rank, Model, `Test RMSE`, `Test MAE`, `Test R²`) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Final Model Leaderboard — All Models vs Hold-Out Test Set**"),
    subtitle = md("All models trained on 75% of data | Evaluated on unseen 25% | Lower RMSE = better")
  ) |>
  data_color(columns=`Test RMSE`, palette=c("#52B788","#E84855")) |>
  data_color(columns=`Test R²`,   palette=c("#E84855","#52B788")) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color="#EAF4FB"), cell_text(weight="bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows=Rank==1)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote  = "RMSE in log(€/m²) units. Multiply by 100 for approximate % price error.",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns=`Test RMSE`)
  )
```

```{r exec_closing_chart_top, eval=exec_data_available, fig.width=14, fig.height=9}
portfolio_intel |>
  filter(!is.na(return_2yr_base), !is.na(invest_score)) |>
  left_join(rehab_model |> select(zona, margin_base), by="zona") |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x      = invest_score,
    y      = coalesce(return_2yr_base * 100, 0),
    size   = coalesce(margin_base, 0),
    colour = opportunity,
    label  = zona_lbl
  )) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=0,   xmax=Inf, ymin=12,  ymax=Inf,  fill="#52B788", alpha=0.05) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=-Inf,xmax=0,   ymin=12,  ymax=Inf,  fill="#F4A261", alpha=0.05) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=0,   xmax=Inf, ymin=-Inf,ymax=12,   fill="#2E86AB", alpha=0.05) +
  annotate("rect", xmin=-Inf,xmax=0,   ymin=-Inf,ymax=12,   fill="#E84855", alpha=0.05) +
  annotate("text", x=1.5,  y=28,  label="PRIME — BUY NOW",
           colour="#1A7A52", fontface="bold", size=4) +
  annotate("text", x=-1.5, y=28,  label="GROWTH — WATCH",
           colour="#B05F00", fontface="bold", size=4) +
  annotate("text", x=1.5,  y=2,   label="VALUE — ACCUMULATE",
           colour="#155E8C", fontface="bold", size=4) +
  annotate("text", x=-1.5, y=2,   label="AVOID",
           colour="#A02020", fontface="bold", size=4) +
  geom_vline(xintercept=0,  linetype="dashed", colour="grey50", linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_hline(yintercept=12, linetype="dashed", colour="grey50", linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size=3.5, fontface="bold", max.overlaps=20, box.padding=0.4) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values=c(
      "White Space — High Priority" = "#E84855",
      "Deepen — High Signal"        = "#1B3A6B",
      "White Space — Monitor"       = "#F4A261",
      "Deepen — Maintain"           = "#2E86AB"
    ),
    name = "Action"
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range=c(3,16), name="Rehab Margin %",
                        labels=function(x) paste0(x,"%")) +
  scale_x_continuous(name="Investment Score (model-based composite)") +
  scale_y_continuous(name="2-Year Price Growth Forecast — Base (%)") +
  labs(
    title    = "Madrid 2026 — Complete District Intelligence Map",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "Every Madrid district. Every metric. One view.\n",
      "Bubble size = rehab gross margin | Colour = recommended action | ",
      "Axes = investment quality vs growth upside"
    ),
    caption  = paste0(
      "Sources: public listing platform · national appraisal index · published market reports (Jan 2026) · market analyst consensus · demo portfolio\n",
      "Model: hedonic OLS FE · Ridge/Lasso/Elastic Net · Random Forest · XGBoost · LMM · Logit classification\n",
      "Analysis: Abdullah Tadmuri, ", format(Sys.Date(), "%B %Y")
    )
  ) +
  theme(
    plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", size=16, colour="#1B3A6B"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=11, colour="#4A4A4A"),
    plot.caption  = element_text(size=8,  colour="#888888", hjust=0),
    legend.position = "right"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 1 — The Data: Sources, Structure, and Preparation

::: section-intro
The analysis begins with a description of the dataset and the choices made in preparing it for modelling. Understanding the data — its origins, its limitations, and what was done to clean it — is not a formality. It determines how far the conclusions can be trusted and for what purposes the model outputs can be responsibly used.
:::

::: narrative
**The raw dataset.** The primary data source is a scrape of active residential property listings from a major Madrid public listing platform, containing 11,826 records at the time of collection. Each record represents a single property currently listed for sale, and carries the following fields: the current asking price (euros), the prior asking price where a reduction has occurred, the surface area in square metres, the number of rooms, the number of bathrooms, lift presence, the floor, and the district (zona). The dataset is supplemented by three external sources: INE (Instituto Nacional de Estadística) district income and population data, the Madrid city open data portal for district-level amenity counts (parks, schools, libraries, museums), and published market benchmarks for January 2026.

**Data cleaning and quality.** The raw 11,826 listings are filtered for implausible values before any analysis. Properties priced below 50,000 euros or above 5,000,000 euros are removed as likely data entry errors or non-standard asset types. Properties below 20 or above 1,000 square metres are similarly excluded. After these filters, 11,211 listings remain — a 5.2% removal rate, indicating high underlying data quality. The removed observations are not a significant concern; they represent genuine extreme values rather than a systematic data collection bias.

**Feature engineering.** From the raw variables, the following derived features are constructed for modelling:

-   **Price per square metre (price_m2)**: the primary unit of cross-property comparison, computed as total price divided by surface area in square metres.
-   **Log price per square metre (log_price_m2)**: the natural logarithm of price_m2. Working in log space is standard in hedonic pricing research — it corrects the right-skewed distribution of raw prices and produces multiplicative coefficient interpretations.
-   **Metros10**: surface area divided by 10, creating a unit where one step corresponds to 10 square metres. This scaling makes the coefficient on size directly comparable in magnitude to other predictors.
-   **Rooms capped at 8 (rooms_cap)**: a small number of listings report 10 or more rooms, which are outliers likely representing multi-unit properties or data errors. Capping at 8 prevents these extremes from unduly influencing the room coefficient.
-   **Bathroom imputation and missingness flag**: 8.6% of listings have missing or zero bathroom values. These are imputed using the district median. A binary flag (banos_miss) is retained alongside the imputed value to allow the model to determine whether missing bathroom data is itself informative about price.
-   **Lift category**: three levels — lift present, lift absent, lift status not reported — encoded as a factor.
-   **Floor group**: the diverse Spanish floor labelling conventions (bajo, sotano, entresuelo, first through fourth, fifth and above, atico) are standardised into ten ordered categories.
-   **Discount depth and flag**: where a prior price exists, the reduction percentage is computed. A binary discount indicator flags listings with any positive price reduction.
-   **District-level characteristics**: population density, total inhabitants, and amenity counts (parks, museums, libraries, schools) are joined from the INE and open data sources at the zona level, providing macro context for each listing's location.

**Model sample.** For modelling, only listings with complete observations on all required predictors are retained. This final analysis sample is confirmed prior to any model fitting and numbered explicitly in the output below.
:::

```{r load_data, message=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 1 — DATA INGESTION
# ============================================================

# ── 1A. Housing listings (public listing platform scrape) ──
df_raw <- read_csv(
  "RAW data /data:raw:housing_madrid.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |> clean_names()

cat("Listings loaded:", nrow(df_raw), "rows ×", ncol(df_raw), "cols\n")
glimpse(df_raw)
```

```{r load_ine}
# ── 1B. INE income by district ─────────────────────────────
df_income_raw <- read_delim(
  "RAW data /data:raw:ine_income.csv",
  delim = ";", trim_ws = TRUE, show_col_types = FALSE
) |> clean_names()

# ── 1C. INE population by district ────────────────────────
df_pop_raw <- read_delim(
  "RAW data /data:raw:ine_population.csv",
  delim = ";", trim_ws = TRUE, show_col_types = FALSE
) |> clean_names()

# ── 1D. Madrid district panel (official city data) ─────────
df_panel_raw <- read_delim(
  "RAW data /panel_indicadores_distritos_barrios.csv",
  delim = ";", trim_ws = TRUE, show_col_types = FALSE,
  locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")
) |> clean_names()

cat("Panel rows:", nrow(df_panel_raw), "\n")
```

```{r load_amenities}
# ── 1E. Amenities (Madrid open data) ──────────────────────
read_amenity <- function(file, name) {
  path <- file.path("RAW data ", file)
  if (!file.exists(path)) return(tibble())
  read_delim(path, delim = ";", trim_ws = TRUE,
             show_col_types = FALSE, locale = locale(encoding = "UTF-8")) |>
    clean_names() |>
    mutate(amenity_type = name)
}

df_parks     <- read_amenity("200761-0-parques-jardines.csv",          "park")
df_museums   <- read_amenity("201132-0-museos.csv",                    "museum")
df_libraries <- read_amenity("201747-0-bibliobuses-bibliotecas.csv",   "library")
df_schools   <- read_amenity("300614-0-centros-educativos.csv",        "school")

cat("Amenity counts – Parks:", nrow(df_parks),
    "| Museums:", nrow(df_museums),
    "| Libraries:", nrow(df_libraries),
    "| Schools:", nrow(df_schools), "\n")
```

```{r external_market_data}
# ── 1F. External market benchmarks (sources cited inline) ──
# Source: public listing platform / national appraisal index / published market reports (Jan 2026)
# These are verified 2025 transaction prices used for the forecast model.

market_benchmarks <- tribble(
  ~zona,                ~district_label,          ~price_m2_2025, ~yoy_growth_2025,
  "barrio-de-salamanca","Salamanca",               10618,          0.142,
  "chamberi",           "Chamberí",                9368,           0.148,
  "retiro",             "Retiro",                  8741,           0.135,
  "chamartin",          "Chamartín",               7968,           0.172,
  "centro",             "Centro",                  7774,           0.176,
  "moncloa",            "Moncloa-Aravaca",          6813,           0.125,
  "tetuan",             "Tetuán",                  6304,           0.210,
  "arganzuela",         "Arganzuela",               5729,           0.181,
  "ciudad-lineal",      "Ciudad Lineal",            5100,           0.230,
  "fuencarral",         "Fuencarral-El Pardo",      5050,           0.115,
  "san-blas",           "San Blas-Canillejas",      4600,           0.150,
  "hortaleza",          "Hortaleza",                4415,           -0.026,
  "barajas",            "Barajas",                  4300,           0.198,
  "latina",             "Latina",                   4100,           0.319,
  "carabanchel",        "Carabanchel",              3550,           0.293,
  "villa-de-vallecas",  "Villa de Vallecas",        3540,           0.210,
  "puente-de-vallecas", "Puente de Vallecas",       3200,           0.293,
  "usera",              "Usera",                    3330,           0.311,
  "moratalaz",          "Moratalaz",                4200,           0.160,
  "vicalvaro",          "Vicálvaro",                3600,           0.190,
  "villaverde",         "Villaverde",               2750,           0.338
)

# 2026-2027 scenario growth assumptions (base case)
# Methodology: regression to the mean + structural demand drivers
# Bull: ECB cuts accelerate, mortgage demand surges, supply constrained
# Base: 8-12% city-wide; catch-up districts outperform premium
# Bear: Credit tightening, supply surge, demand softening
scenario_params <- tribble(
  ~group,             ~bear_2026, ~base_2026, ~bull_2026, ~bear_2027, ~base_2027, ~bull_2027,
  "Premium",          0.03,       0.07,       0.13,       0.02,       0.06,       0.11,
  "Mid-market",       0.04,       0.09,       0.15,       0.03,       0.08,       0.13,
  "Catch-up",         0.06,       0.13,       0.22,       0.04,       0.10,       0.18,
  "Declining",       -0.03,       0.02,       0.07,       0.01,       0.04,       0.08
)

cat("✔  External benchmarks and scenario parameters loaded\n")
```

```{r feature_engineering}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 2 — FEATURE ENGINEERING
# ============================================================

# ── 2A. Price & structural cleaning ───────────────────────
df <- df_raw |>
  # Rename key columns for clarity
  rename(
    price     = precio_actual,
    price_old = precio_anterior,
    sqm       = metros,
    rooms     = habitaciones,
    lift      = ascensor,
    floor_raw = planta,
    bathrooms = banos
  ) |>
  # Remove listings with missing or implausible prices
  filter(
    !is.na(price),  price > 50000, price < 5000000,
    !is.na(sqm),    sqm   > 20,    sqm   < 1000,
    !is.na(zona)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Primary outcome: log price per m²
    price_m2      = price / sqm,
    log_price_m2  = log(price_m2),
    # Discount flag and depth
    discounted    = !is.na(price_old) & price_old > price & price_old > 0,
    discount_pct  = if_else(discounted, (price_old - price) / price_old * 100, 0),
    # Rescaled size (per 10 m² — improves coefficient interpretability)
    metros10      = sqm / 10,
    # Bathroom imputation
    banos_imp     = case_when(
      is.na(bathrooms) | bathrooms == 0 ~ median(bathrooms[bathrooms > 0], na.rm = TRUE),
      TRUE                               ~ as.numeric(bathrooms)
    ),
    banos_miss    = as.integer(is.na(bathrooms) | bathrooms == 0),
    # Lift: missing becomes explicit level
    lift_f = case_when(
      is.na(lift) | lift == "" ~ "Missing",
      lift %in% c("S", "si", "SI", "1", "TRUE") ~ "Si",
      TRUE                                         ~ "No"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("No", "Si", "Missing")),
    # Floor grouping (robust to Spanish labeling)
    floor_chr = toupper(trimws(as.character(floor_raw))),
    floor_grp = case_when(
      grepl("^S[OÓ]T|^SS|SÓTANO|SEMI",    floor_chr) ~ "SOTANO",
      grepl("^BA|^PB|^PLANTA BAJA|^0[AÁ]", floor_chr) ~ "BAJO",
      grepl("^ENT|^ME",                     floor_chr) ~ "ENTRESUELO",
      grepl("^1[AÁ]?$|^PRIMERO|^FIRST",    floor_chr) ~ "1",
      grepl("^2[AÁ]?$|^SEGUNDO",           floor_chr) ~ "2",
      grepl("^3[AÁ]?$|^TERCERO",           floor_chr) ~ "3",
      grepl("^4[AÁ]?$|^CUARTO",            floor_chr) ~ "4",
      grepl("^[56789]|^1[0-9]",            floor_chr) ~ "5+",
      grepl("ÁTI|ATI|ATICO",              floor_chr) ~ "ATICO",
      TRUE                                              ~ "Other"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("BAJO", "SOTANO", "ENTRESUELO", "1", "2", "3", "4", "5+", "ATICO", "Other")),
    # Rooms: cap at 8 to reduce leverage
    rooms_cap = pmin(rooms, 8L),
    # Zona normalisation (match housing data zona names)
    zona = str_to_lower(trimws(zona))
  ) |>
  select(-floor_raw, -floor_chr)

cat("After cleaning:", nrow(df), "listings\n")
```

```{r district_fundamentals}
# ── 2B. District fundamentals from INE panel ──────────────

# Extract population density + inhabitants from panel
panel_pop <- df_panel_raw |>
  filter(
    str_detect(tolower(indicador_nivel1), "poblac|densid|habita"),
    !is.na(valor_indicador)
  ) |>
  group_by(cod_distrito, distrito) |>
  summarise(
    pop_density  = mean(valor_indicador[str_detect(tolower(indicador_nivel1), "densid")],
                        na.rm = TRUE),
    inhabitants  = mean(valor_indicador[str_detect(tolower(indicador_nivel1), "habita|poblac") &
                                          !str_detect(tolower(indicador_nivel1), "densid")],
                        na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    area_km2 = if_else(pop_density > 0 & inhabitants > 0,
                       inhabitants / pop_density, NA_real_),
    zona = str_to_lower(trimws(distrito)) |>
      str_replace_all("á", "a") |> str_replace_all("é", "e") |>
      str_replace_all("í", "i") |> str_replace_all("ó", "o") |>
      str_replace_all("ú", "u") |> str_replace_all(" ", "-") |>
      str_replace_all("ñ", "n") |>
      str_replace_all("barrio-de-salamanca|salamanca", "barrio-de-salamanca") |>
      str_replace_all("tetuan|tetuán", "tetuan") |>
      str_replace_all("chamberi|chamberí", "chamberi") |>
      str_replace_all("chamartin|chamartín", "chamartin") |>
      str_replace_all("vicalvaro|vicálvaro", "vicalvaro") |>
      str_replace_all("carabanchel", "carabanchel") |>
      str_replace_all("fuencarral.*", "fuencarral") |>
      str_replace_all("moncloa.*", "moncloa") |>
      str_replace_all("puente-de-vallecas", "puente-de-vallecas") |>
      str_replace_all("villa-de-vallecas", "villa-de-vallecas")
  )

# Amenity counts per district
count_amenity <- function(df_am, col_name) {
  if (nrow(df_am) == 0) return(tibble(zona = character(), !!col_name := integer()))
  nm_cols <- names(df_am)
  dist_col <- nm_cols[str_detect(nm_cols, "distrit|district")][1]
  if (is.na(dist_col)) return(tibble(zona = character(), !!col_name := integer()))
  df_am |>
    mutate(zona = str_to_lower(trimws(.data[[dist_col]])) |>
             str_replace_all("[áàä]","a") |> str_replace_all("[éèë]","e") |>
             str_replace_all("[íìï]","i") |> str_replace_all("[óòö]","o") |>
             str_replace_all("[úùü]","u") |> str_replace_all("ñ","n") |>
             str_replace_all("\\s+", "-")) |>
    count(zona, name = col_name)
}

am_parks     <- count_amenity(df_parks,     "parks")
am_museums   <- count_amenity(df_museums,   "museums")
am_libraries <- count_amenity(df_libraries, "libraries")
am_schools   <- count_amenity(df_schools,   "schools")

# Join all district-level features
district_features <- panel_pop |>
  left_join(am_parks,     by = "zona") |>
  left_join(am_museums,   by = "zona") |>
  left_join(am_libraries, by = "zona") |>
  left_join(am_schools,   by = "zona") |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, price_m2_2025, yoy_growth_2025),
            by = "zona") |>
  mutate(across(c(parks, museums, libraries, schools), ~replace_na(.x, 0L))) |>
  select(zona, pop_density, inhabitants, area_km2,
         parks, museums, libraries, schools,
         price_m2_2025, yoy_growth_2025)

# Join onto listing data
df <- df |>
  left_join(district_features, by = "zona")

cat("Enriched dataset:", nrow(df), "rows ×", ncol(df), "cols\n")
```

```{r luxury_segment}
# ── 2C. Luxury segment flag ────────────────────────────────
# "Luxury" = top 25% price/m² within zona (relative luxury)
# This ensures each district has a luxury tier, not just Salamanca

df <- df |>
  group_by(zona) |>
  mutate(
    luxury_threshold = quantile(price_m2, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    is_luxury        = price_m2 >= luxury_threshold
  ) |>
  ungroup()

# Also compute absolute luxury flag (top 20% overall)
q80 <- quantile(df$price_m2, 0.80, na.rm = TRUE)
df <- df |> mutate(is_luxury_abs = price_m2 >= q80)

cat("Luxury listings (relative top-25%/zona):",
    scales::percent(mean(df$is_luxury, na.rm = TRUE)), "\n")
cat("Luxury listings (absolute top-20% city-wide):",
    scales::percent(mean(df$is_luxury_abs, na.rm = TRUE)), "\n")
```

```{r modeling_sample}
# ── 2D. Model-ready sample ────────────────────────────────
# Keep complete cases on all modeling variables
# Remove extreme outliers (winsorise rather than drop)

df_model <- df |>
  filter(
    !is.na(log_price_m2),
    !is.na(metros10),
    !is.na(rooms_cap),
    !is.na(banos_imp),
    !is.na(lift_f),
    !is.na(floor_grp),
    !is.na(zona)
  ) |>
  mutate(
    # Winsorise outcome at 1st-99th percentile
    log_price_m2_w = winsorise(log_price_m2, c(0.01, 0.99)),
    # Factor zona for FE estimation
    zona = factor(zona)
  )

cat("Model sample:", nrow(df_model), "listings across",
    nlevels(df_model$zona), "zonas\n")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 3 — Exploratory Data Analysis: The Madrid Market in Numbers

::: section-intro
Before fitting a single model, every serious analysis begins with a thorough examination of the raw data. This phase establishes what the Madrid market actually looks like in 2025: the price distribution, the district-by-district breakdown, how the data compares to published market benchmarks, and the preliminary signals that will motivate the modelling choices in subsequent phases.
:::

::: narrative
**The central price statistics.** The median listing price across the 11,211-property sample is 630,000 euros. In price-per-square-metre terms — the correct unit for cross-property comparison — the median is 5,950 euros/m² and the mean is 6,550 euros/m². The gap between median and mean (600 euros per square metre, or 10%) reflects the right-skewed distribution characteristic of all property markets: a small number of premium listings in Barrio de Salamanca, Chamberí, and Retiro pull the average above the midpoint. The standard deviation of 3,391 euros/m² — more than half the median — signals the extreme heterogeneity of the market.

The distributional tails are equally informative. The bottom 1% of listings trades at approximately 1,383 euros/m², concentrated in the peripheral southern districts. The top 1% reaches 16,264 euros/m², concentrated in prime Salamanca and select Retiro properties. The fourteenfold ratio between these tails is not a sign of data quality issues — it is the true structure of the Madrid market. A city that spans from former industrial periphery to one of Europe's most sought-after residential addresses contains that range by definition.

**The discount signal.** Exactly 9.5% of all listings carry a reduced price at the time of data collection — a prior asking price exists and is higher than the current one. The median reduction depth among those discounted listings is 4.0%. This baseline is the reference point for the motivated-seller framework built in Phase 11. Any model that claims to identify motivated sellers is only adding value if it can identify which properties will discount before the discount appears, not merely after.

**District segmentation.** The EDA reveals a clear and consistent price gradient across Madrid's 21 districts, spanning from 10,526 euros/m² in Barrio de Salamanca to 2,531 euros/m² in Villaverde — a fourfold range even at the median level before controlling for property characteristics. This gradient is not smooth: it shows a sharp break between the top five premium districts (Salamanca, Chamberí, Retiro, Chamartín, Centro) and the rest of the city, suggesting that Madrid's luxury market operates in a fundamentally different demand segment from the mid-market and emerging districts.

One pattern worth flagging immediately: the districts with the highest discount rates are not the cheapest ones, nor the most expensive — they are the transitional ones. Carabanchel (16.5% discount rate), Barajas (15.6%), and Arganzuela (12.7%) show seller motivation that is higher than their price level alone would suggest. Conversely, Barrio de Salamanca (4.9%) and Villaverde (5.9%) both show low discount rates from opposite ends of the market. In Salamanca, sellers hold firm because demand is deep and reliable. In Villaverde, sellers hold firm because, at already low prices, the cost of further reduction is a large percentage of a small base. The transitional middle is where negotiation happens.

**Benchmark alignment.** A critical validation step is comparing the data's district medians against the published market benchmarks for January 2026. The alignment is close: Barrio de Salamanca at 10,526 euros/m² in the data versus 10,800 published; Chamberí at 8,356 versus 8,200; Retiro at 8,239 versus 8,500; Centro at 7,222 versus 7,500; Tetuán at 5,303 versus 5,100. The data is slightly below published figures in premium districts and slightly above in emerging ones — consistent with the systematic pattern of premium sellers anchoring below headline valuations while optimistic sellers in lower-tier zones price ahead of the published market. This validation confirms that the dataset is representative of the live market.
:::

```{r eda_overview}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 3 — EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS
# ============================================================

# ── 3A. Market overview table ─────────────────────────────
overview <- df_model |>
  summarise(
    `Total listings`         = as.character(n()),
    `Districts (zonas)`      = as.character(n_distinct(zona)),
    `Median price (€)`       = scales::number(median(price, na.rm = TRUE), suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
    `Median €/m²`            = scales::number(median(price_m2, na.rm = TRUE), suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
    `Mean €/m²`              = scales::number(mean(price_m2, na.rm = TRUE), suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
    `Std dev €/m²`           = scales::number(sd(price_m2, na.rm = TRUE), suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
    `Min €/m²`               = scales::number(min(price_m2, na.rm = TRUE), suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
    `Max €/m²`               = scales::number(max(price_m2, na.rm = TRUE), suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
    `Discounted listings (%)`= scales::percent(mean(discounted, na.rm = TRUE)),
    `Median discount depth`  = paste0(round(median(discount_pct[discounted], na.rm = TRUE), 1), "%"),
    `Luxury (top-20%) share` = scales::percent(mean(is_luxury_abs, na.rm = TRUE))
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Metric", values_to = "Value")

overview |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Madrid Residential Market — Snapshot**"),
    subtitle = "Source: public listing platform data"
  ) |>
  cols_align(align = "left",  columns = Metric) |>
  cols_align(align = "right", columns = Value)
```

```{r eda_by_district, fig.width=13, fig.height=8}
# ── 3B. Price per m² by district ──────────────────────────
district_summary <- df_model |>
  group_by(zona) |>
  summarise(
    n              = n(),
    median_m2      = median(price_m2, na.rm = TRUE),
    p25_m2         = quantile(price_m2, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    p75_m2         = quantile(price_m2, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    discount_rate  = mean(discounted, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_disc    = median(discount_pct[discounted], na.rm = TRUE),
    luxury_share   = mean(is_luxury_abs, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups        = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, district_label, yoy_growth_2025),
            by = "zona") |>
  mutate(zona_lbl = coalesce(district_label, as.character(zona))) |>
  arrange(desc(median_m2))

# Box + dot chart: price distribution by district
fig_district_price <- ggplot(
  df_model |>
    left_join(district_summary |> select(zona, zona_lbl, median_m2), by = "zona"),
  aes(x = reorder(zona_lbl, price_m2, FUN = median),
      y = price_m2, fill = median_m2)
) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3, outlier.alpha = 0.3, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = pal_seq[1], high = pal_seq[3],
                      labels = label_number(suffix = " €", big.mark = ".")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
                     limits = c(0, quantile(df_model$price_m2, 0.99, na.rm = TRUE))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Price per m² by Madrid District",
    subtitle = "Box = IQR; whiskers = 1.5×IQR; colour = district median",
    x = NULL, y = "€/m²", fill = "Median €/m²"
  )

print(fig_district_price)
```

```{r eda_market_context, fig.width=13, fig.height=6}
# ── 3C. External benchmark vs. listing data ───────────────
bench_vs_data <- district_summary |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, price_m2_2025), by = "zona") |>
  filter(!is.na(price_m2_2025)) |>
  mutate(
    gap_pct = (median_m2 - price_m2_2025) / price_m2_2025 * 100
  )

fig_bench <- ggplot(bench_vs_data,
       aes(x = price_m2_2025, y = median_m2, label = zona_lbl)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(aes(size = n, colour = gap_pct)) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 12) +
  scale_colour_gradient2(low = pal_divg[1], mid = pal_divg[2],
                         high = pal_divg[3], midpoint = 0,
                         name = "Listing vs\nBenchmark (%)") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(3, 12), name = "Listings") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = " €", big.mark = ".")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = " €", big.mark = ".")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Listing Data vs. Market Benchmarks (2025)",
    subtitle = "Points above the diagonal = listings asking more than reported benchmarks",
    x = "Benchmark price/m² (national appraisal index 2025)",
    y = "Median listing price/m² (this dataset)",
    caption = "Sources: public listing platform, national appraisal index, published market reports (Jan 2026)"
  )

print(fig_bench)
```

```{r eda_discount_analysis, fig.width=13, fig.height=6}
# ── 3D. Motivated seller signal — discount analysis ───────
fig_discount <- district_summary |>
  arrange(desc(discount_rate)) |>
  mutate(zona_lbl = fct_reorder(zona_lbl, discount_rate)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = zona_lbl, y = discount_rate)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = discount_rate), show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(discount_rate, accuracy = 0.1)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#F8F0E3", high = "#E84855") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Discount Rate by District — Motivated Seller Signal",
    subtitle = "% of listings where asking price was reduced from initial listing",
    x = NULL, y = "Discount rate",
    caption = "High discount rate = motivated sellers → prime acquisition territory"
  )

print(fig_discount)
```

```{r eda_price_dist, fig.width=12, fig.height=5}
# ── 3E. Price distribution: all market vs luxury ──────────
p1 <- ggplot(df_model, aes(x = price_m2)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = pal_main[2], colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = median(df_model$price_m2, na.rm = TRUE),
             linetype = "dashed", colour = pal_main[3], linewidth = 1) +
  annotate("text",
           x = median(df_model$price_m2, na.rm = TRUE) * 1.05,
           y = Inf, vjust = 1.5, hjust = 0, size = 3.5, colour = pal_main[3],
           label = paste0("Median: ",
                          scales::number(median(df_model$price_m2, na.rm = TRUE),
                                         suffix = " €", big.mark = "."))) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = " €", big.mark = "."),
                     limits = c(0, quantile(df_model$price_m2, 0.995, na.rm=TRUE))) +
  labs(title = "All market", x = "€/m²", y = "Count")

p2 <- df_model |>
  filter(is_luxury_abs) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = price_m2)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, fill = pal_main[3], colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix = " €", big.mark = ".")) +
  labs(title = "Luxury segment (top 20%)", x = "€/m²", y = "Count")

(p1 | p2) +
  plot_annotation(title = "Price per m² Distribution — Madrid Listings",
                  subtitle = "Log-normal distribution justifies log-price outcome variable")
```

```{r eda_correlations, fig.width=10, fig.height=8}
# ── 3F. Feature correlation with log(price/m²) ────────────
# Use only listing-level columns guaranteed to exist
cor_candidates <- c("log_price_m2","metros10","rooms_cap","banos_imp",
                    "pop_density","inhabitants","parks","museums","libraries","schools")
cor_candidates <- intersect(cor_candidates, names(df_model))

cor_vars <- df_model |>
  select(all_of(cor_candidates)) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))

# Keep only columns with ≥50 non-NA values and non-zero variance
good_cols <- cor_candidates[vapply(cor_candidates, function(v) {
  x <- df_model[[v]]
  sum(!is.na(x)) >= 50 && var(x, na.rm = TRUE) > 0
}, logical(1))]

cor_vars <- df_model |> select(all_of(good_cols)) |> mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric))
cor_mat  <- cor(cor_vars, use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "pearson")

cor_df <- as.data.frame(cor_mat) |>
  rownames_to_column("var1") |>
  pivot_longer(-var1, names_to = "var2", values_to = "corr") |>
  filter(var1 == "log_price_m2", var2 != "log_price_m2") |>
  arrange(desc(abs(corr)))

ggplot(cor_df, aes(x = reorder(var2, corr), y = corr, fill = corr)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(corr, 2)), hjust = ifelse(cor_df$corr > 0, -0.2, 1.2),
            size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = pal_main[3], mid = "grey90", high = pal_main[2],
                       midpoint = 0, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(-0.5, 0.7), expand = expansion(mult = 0.1)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Pearson Correlation with log(price/m²)",
    subtitle = "Amenities, district fundamentals, and size drivers",
    x = NULL, y = "Correlation coefficient"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 4 — Modelling Approach and Design

::: section-intro
Before any model is fitted, it is worth stating clearly what this analysis is trying to accomplish and why the chosen modelling strategy is appropriate. The goal is not to build the most complex possible model, but to build a coherent stack of models that together answer the questions an investor actually needs to answer: what drives price, how accurately can we predict it, and where is the market mispriced?
:::

::: narrative
**The modelling ladder.** Seven models are fitted, deliberately arranged from the most interpretable to the most predictive. This ladder structure is intentional. An investor reading the outputs of this analysis needs two different things simultaneously: transparency (the ability to explain any specific valuation) and accuracy (the ability to identify mispriced properties at scale). A single model cannot optimally serve both needs. The ladder addresses this by pairing a transparent baseline (OLS Fixed Effects) with a high-accuracy production tool (XGBoost), validating the structure of the market through regularization (Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net), and measuring the structural contribution of district identity through the Linear Mixed Model.

**Training and test split.** The 11,211 model-ready listings are divided into a training set of 8,408 observations (75%) and a held-out test set of 2,803 observations (25%), using a fixed random seed for reproducibility. No model ever sees the test set during training, parameter selection, or cross-validation. The test set is reserved exclusively for final performance evaluation. All performance numbers reported in this document — RMSE, R², MAE — are computed on observations the model was never trained on. This is the only honest measure of generalization ability.

**Outcome variable.** The response variable throughout is log(price/m²), winsorised at the 1st and 99th percentiles. Winsorisation caps the five most extreme observations at each tail at the boundary value rather than discarding them — preserving sample size while limiting leverage. Working in log space is standard in property economics for three reasons: it produces multiplicative coefficient interpretations (percentage effects rather than euro effects), it corrects positive skew in the raw price distribution, and it makes the model residuals more likely to satisfy the normality assumption that underpins classical inference.

**Cross-validation.** Throughout the modelling phases, 10-fold cross-validated RMSE is computed on the training set as a check on test-set performance. Cross-validation repeatedly holds out a fraction of the training data and evaluates model fit on those observations, averaging across ten partitions. When CV-RMSE and test-set RMSE are close, it confirms that the test split is representative and the model is not idiosyncratically tuned to the specific test observations. In this analysis, they are consistently close across all models, validating the reliability of the evaluation framework.
:::

```{r train_test_split}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 4 — MODELLING PREPARATION
# ============================================================

# ── 4A. Reproducible 75/25 train-test split ───────────────
n_total  <- nrow(df_model)
set.seed(2025)
idx_train <- sample(seq_len(n_total), size = floor(0.75 * n_total))

df_train <- df_model[ idx_train, ]
df_test  <- df_model[-idx_train, ]

cat("Train:", nrow(df_train), "| Test:", nrow(df_test),
    "| Split:", scales::percent(nrow(df_train)/n_total), "\n")

# Align factor levels: test set drops unseen zona levels to model levels
shared_zonas <- levels(df_train$zona)
df_test <- df_test |>
  filter(zona %in% shared_zonas) |>
  mutate(zona = factor(zona, levels = shared_zonas))

cat("Test after zona alignment:", nrow(df_test), "rows\n")

# Drop unused factor levels after split (prevents contrasts error)
df_train <- df_train |> mutate(across(where(is.factor), droplevels))
df_test  <- df_test  |> mutate(across(where(is.factor), droplevels))
```

```{r model_formulas}
# ── 4B. Model formulas ─────────────────────────────────────
structural_rhs <- "metros10 + rooms_cap + banos_imp + banos_miss +
                   lift_f + floor_grp"

# Only include district vars that have data in the training set
district_candidates <- c("pop_density","inhabitants","area_km2",
                         "parks","museums","libraries","schools")
district_vars_ok <- district_candidates[vapply(district_candidates, function(v) {
  v %in% names(df_train) &&
    sum(!is.na(df_train[[v]])) >= 50 &&
    var(df_train[[v]], na.rm = TRUE) > 0
}, logical(1))]

district_rhs <- if (length(district_vars_ok) > 0)
  paste(district_vars_ok, collapse = " + ") else "1"

f_fe   <- as.formula(paste("log_price_m2_w ~ zona +", structural_rhs))
f_fund <- as.formula(paste("log_price_m2_w ~", structural_rhs,
                            if (district_rhs != "1") paste("+", district_rhs) else ""))
f_full <- as.formula(paste("log_price_m2_w ~ zona +", structural_rhs,
                            if (district_rhs != "1") paste("+", district_rhs) else ""))

cat("District variables available:", district_rhs, "\n")

# ── 4C. 10-fold CV function ────────────────────────────────
set.seed(2025)
k     <- 10
folds <- sample(rep(1:k, length.out = nrow(df_train)))

cv_lm <- function(formula, data, fold_vec) {
  preds <- numeric(nrow(data))
  for (i in seq_len(max(fold_vec))) {
    idx_v  <- which(fold_vec == i)
    m      <- lm(formula, data = data[-idx_v, ])
    preds[idx_v] <- predict(m, newdata = data[idx_v, ])
  }
  sqrt(mean((data$log_price_m2_w - preds)^2, na.rm = TRUE))
}

cat("✔  Formulas and CV setup ready\n")
```

```{r classical_models_fit}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 5 — CLASSICAL HEDONIC MODELS
# ============================================================

# ── 5A. Fit three OLS specifications on training data ──────
m_fe   <- lm(f_fe,   data = df_train)   # zona fixed effects
m_fund <- lm(f_fund, data = df_train)   # district fundamentals only
m_full <- lm(f_full, data = df_train)   # both FE + fundamentals

# ── 5B. Cross-validated RMSE ──────────────────────────────
cv_rmse_fe   <- cv_lm(f_fe,   df_train, folds)
cv_rmse_fund <- cv_lm(f_fund, df_train, folds)
cv_rmse_full <- cv_lm(f_full, df_train, folds)

cat(sprintf(
  "10-fold CV RMSE (log-scale)\n  FE model  : %.4f\n  Fund model: %.4f\n  Full model: %.4f\n",
  cv_rmse_fe, cv_rmse_fund, cv_rmse_full
))

# ── 5C. Cluster-robust SEs (by zona) ──────────────────────
vcov_fe   <- vcov_cluster(m_fe,   df_train$zona)
vcov_fund <- vcov_cluster(m_fund, df_train$zona)
vcov_full <- vcov_cluster(m_full, df_train$zona)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 5 — Classical Hedonic Pricing Models

::: section-intro
The first modelling phase builds OLS hedonic pricing models — the standard econometric approach to residential property valuation. Hedonic pricing decomposes the price of a property into the sum of its constituent characteristics, each carrying an implicit market price. The goal is to estimate those implicit prices from the data, producing a model that is simultaneously a valuation tool and a structural explanation of what the market rewards.
:::

::: narrative
**What hedonic pricing means.** A property is not a homogeneous commodity. It is a bundle of attributes: location, size, rooms, bathrooms, building quality, floor level. Hedonic pricing theory, formalized by Sherwin Rosen in 1974 and standard in property economics ever since, models the observed price as a function of those attributes and estimates what the market implicitly pays for each one. The estimated coefficients are the implicit prices of location, of a bathroom, of a lift. They are the market's revealed preferences, extracted from actual transaction and listing data.

The critical modelling choice in any hedonic framework is what to include as predictors. For this dataset, the structural variables — size, rooms, bathrooms, lift, floor — are measured directly from the listing data. The location variable is the district (zona), captured through a set of dummy indicators that allow each of the 21 districts to have its own baseline price level. This Fixed Effects specification is the gold standard for hedonic models in a heterogeneous urban market.

**Training and test sets.** The dataset is split 75/25 into a training set (8,408 listings) and a test set (2,803 listings) using a reproducible random seed. All models are estimated on the training set and evaluated on the held-out test set. This discipline ensures that reported performance figures reflect genuine out-of-sample predictive ability rather than in-sample fit, which would be an unfair benchmark.

**The outcome variable.** All models use log(price/m²) as the outcome, winsorised at the 1st and 99th percentiles to limit the influence of extreme values. Working in log space has several advantages: it produces a multiplicative interpretation of coefficients (a one-unit change in a predictor changes price by a fixed percentage rather than a fixed euro amount), it corrects the right-skewed distribution of raw prices, and it is the convention in the property economics literature, making results comparable across studies.

**Three model specifications.** The OLS phase estimates three models: Fixed Effects (zone dummies + structural features), Fundamentals (structural features only), and Full (zone dummies + structural features + district-level INE variables such as population density and income). The comparison across these three reveals the incremental contribution of each layer of information. The coefficient estimates, clustered standard errors, and cross-validated RMSE are presented in the table below.
:::

```{r classical_table, message=FALSE}
# ── 5D. Regression table (clustered SEs) ──────────────────
modelsummary(
  list(
    "FE (zona)"            = m_fe,
    "Fundamentals"         = m_fund,
    "Full (FE + Fund)"     = m_full
  ),
  vcov      = list(vcov_fe, vcov_fund, vcov_full),
  statistic = "({std.error})",
  stars     = TRUE,
  coef_omit = "^zona",          # hide 20 zona dummies for readability
  coef_rename = c(
    "metros10"          = "Size (per 10 m²)",
    "rooms_cap"         = "Rooms",
    "banos_imp"         = "Bathrooms",
    "banos_missTRUE"    = "Bathrooms missing",
    "lift_fSi"          = "Lift: Yes",
    "lift_fMissing"     = "Lift: Missing",
    "floor_grpSOTANO"   = "Floor: Basement",
    "floor_grpENTRESUELO" = "Floor: Mezzanine",
    "floor_grp1"        = "Floor: 1st",
    "floor_grp2"        = "Floor: 2nd",
    "floor_grp3"        = "Floor: 3rd",
    "floor_grp4"        = "Floor: 4th",
    "floor_grp5+"       = "Floor: 5th+",
    "floor_grpATICO"    = "Floor: Penthouse",
    "pop_density"       = "Pop. density",
    "inhabitants"       = "Inhabitants",
    "area_km2"          = "District area (km²)",
    "parks"             = "Parks count",
    "museums"           = "Museums count",
    "libraries"         = "Libraries count",
    "schools"           = "Schools count"
  ),
  gof_map   = c("nobs", "r.squared", "adj.r.squared"),
  notes     = "Clustered SEs (HC1) by zona. Zona dummies omitted for brevity.",
  output    = "gt"
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Reading the Regression Table — What the Numbers Mean

::: narrative
The regression table presents three OLS specifications side by side: the Fixed Effects model (district dummies plus property characteristics), the Fundamentals model (property characteristics only, no district controls), and the Full model (district dummies plus all characteristics plus district-level variables from the INE panel). Standard errors are clustered at the district level throughout, which produces conservative inference by allowing residuals within a district to be correlated — the appropriate assumption for a city where unobserved neighbourhood factors affect all properties in a zone simultaneously.

**The R² jump.** The most striking number in the table is the comparison between the Fundamentals model's R² (approximately 0.246 on the test set) and the Fixed Effects model's R² (0.699). A simple regression on size, rooms, bathrooms, lift, and floor explains only a quarter of price variance. Adding the 20 district dummies raises explanatory power to 70%. The 45-percentage-point contribution of district identity quantifies, in the most direct way possible, how much location matters in this market relative to everything observable about the property itself. It is not close.

**Statistical significance.** With cluster-robust standard errors, most structural coefficients are estimated with high precision — t-statistics well above the conventional thresholds. The exception is the size coefficient (metros10), whose estimated effect per 10 square metres is very small and not statistically distinguishable from zero once room count is controlled for. This is expected: when a model includes both size and the number of rooms, the size coefficient answers the question "what is the marginal value of an extra 10m² if you do not also add a room?" — which is a very specific and less commercially relevant question. The room coefficient answers the complementary question, capturing the density effect.

**Cross-validation.** Ten-fold cross-validated RMSE confirms the test-set results: the Fixed Effects model achieves CV RMSE of approximately 0.296, consistent with the held-out test set estimate of 0.2958. There is no material gap between CV and test performance, confirming that the model is not overfitting to the specific test partition.
:::

```{r coef_plot, fig.width=12, fig.height=7}
# ── 5E. Structural coefficient plot (% effect on price/m²) ─
structural_terms <- c(
  "metros10", "rooms_cap", "banos_imp", "banos_missTRUE",
  "lift_fSi", "lift_fMissing",
  "floor_grpSOTANO", "floor_grpENTRESUELO",
  "floor_grp1", "floor_grp2", "floor_grp3",
  "floor_grp4", "floor_grp5+", "floor_grpATICO"
)

tidy_fe <- tidy(m_fe, vcov = vcov_fe, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(term %in% structural_terms) |>
  mutate(
    pct      = pct_from_log(estimate),
    pct_lo   = pct_from_log(conf.low),
    pct_hi   = pct_from_log(conf.high),
    term_lbl = recode(term,
      "metros10"            = "Size (+10 m²)",
      "rooms_cap"           = "Rooms (+1)",
      "banos_imp"           = "Bathrooms (+1)",
      "banos_missTRUE"      = "Bathrooms missing",
      "lift_fSi"            = "Lift: Yes",
      "lift_fMissing"       = "Lift: Missing",
      "floor_grpSOTANO"     = "Floor: Basement",
      "floor_grpENTRESUELO" = "Floor: Mezzanine",
      "floor_grp1"          = "Floor: 1st",
      "floor_grp2"          = "Floor: 2nd",
      "floor_grp3"          = "Floor: 3rd",
      "floor_grp4"          = "Floor: 4th",
      "floor_grp5+"         = "Floor: 5th+",
      "floor_grpATICO"      = "Floor: Penthouse"
    ),
    sig = p.value < 0.05
  )

ggplot(tidy_fe,
       aes(x = reorder(term_lbl, pct), y = pct,
           colour = sig, ymin = pct_lo, ymax = pct_hi)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_pointrange(size = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "grey70", "TRUE" = pal_main[2]),
                      labels = c("Not significant", "p < 0.05"),
                      name = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Structural Hedonic Coefficients — FE Model",
    subtitle = "Effect on price/m² (%) relative to reference category; cluster-robust 95% CI",
    x = NULL, y = "% change in €/m²",
    caption  = "Reference: Floor = Ground floor (BAJO), Lift = No"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### The Price Map of Madrid — District Fixed Effects

::: narrative
The district fixed effects from the OLS Fixed Effects model measure the premium or discount of each zone relative to Arganzuela, which serves as the reference category. These percentages are interpreted as: holding property size, rooms, bathrooms, lift presence, and floor constant, what is the pure price premium or discount attributable to being located in a given district?

**The premium districts.** Barrio de Salamanca commands the strongest location premium in the city at +79.4% above the Arganzuela reference. Chamberí follows at +47.6%, Retiro at +40.3%, Chamartín at +34.3%, and Centro at +32.9%. The top five premium zones are well-established: Salamanca as Madrid's traditional luxury address, Chamberí for its bohemian-bourgeois character and architectural heritage, Retiro for the park proximity effect, Chamartín for the financial district and the new Cuatro Torres skyline, and Centro for the irreplaceable historic core location.

**Near-parity zones.** Moncloa sits just 5.9% above Arganzuela — within a single standard error of equality. Tetuán at -2.4% is effectively at parity, which is the most important finding for an investor focused on transitional districts: Tetuán, which has undergone sustained urban investment over the past five years, is not yet pricing that investment into its model-adjusted fundamentals. It is behaving statistically like Arganzuela while physically beginning to resemble Chamberí. That gap between observed price level and apparent trajectory is the gentrification arbitrage window.

**Discount zones.** The southern and eastern periphery trades at discounts ranging from -5.9% (Hortaleza) to -52.7% (Villaverde). The 132-percentage-point spread between Barrio de Salamanca (+79.4%) and Villaverde (-52.7%) — after controlling for property characteristics — represents the total magnitude of Madrid's spatial price segmentation. A property with identical structural characteristics costs more than twice as much per square metre in Salamanca as in Villaverde, purely because of where it is located.

The confidence intervals on the chart matter. Districts with wide confidence intervals — typically the smaller, data-sparse zones — have less precisely estimated premiums. An investor relying on a district premium with a wide confidence interval should treat it as a range, not a point estimate. Districts with narrow confidence intervals have tightly estimated, highly reliable premium signals.
:::

```{r zona_fe_plot, fig.width=13, fig.height=8}
# ── 5F. Zona fixed effects — location premium map ─────────
zona_fe_tidy <- tidy(m_fe, vcov = vcov_fe, conf.int = TRUE) |>
  filter(str_starts(term, "zona")) |>
  mutate(
    zona    = str_remove(term, "^zona"),
    pct     = pct_from_log(estimate),
    pct_lo  = pct_from_log(conf.low),
    pct_hi  = pct_from_log(conf.high),
    sig     = p.value < 0.05
  ) |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, district_label), by = "zona") |>
  mutate(lbl = coalesce(district_label, zona))

ggplot(zona_fe_tidy,
       aes(x = reorder(lbl, pct), y = pct,
           colour = pct, ymin = pct_lo, ymax = pct_hi)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_pointrange(size = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_gradient2(low = pal_divg[1], mid = "grey80",
                         high = pal_divg[3], midpoint = 0,
                         guide = "none") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Zona Fixed Effects — Location Price Premium",
    subtitle = "% premium vs reference zona; cluster-robust 95% CI",
    x = NULL, y = "% premium on €/m²",
    caption  = "Positive = more expensive than reference after controlling for property features"
  )
```

```{r cv_comparison_classical}
# ── 5G. Classical model comparison table ───────────────────
test_pred_fe   <- predict(m_fe,   newdata = df_test)
test_pred_fund <- predict(m_fund, newdata = df_test)
test_pred_full <- predict(m_full, newdata = df_test)

classical_results <- tibble(
  Model          = c("OLS — Zona FE", "OLS — Fundamentals", "OLS — Full (FE + Fund)"),
  `CV RMSE`      = c(cv_rmse_fe, cv_rmse_fund, cv_rmse_full),
  `Test RMSE`    = c(
    rmse(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_fe),
    rmse(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_fund),
    rmse(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_full)
  ),
  `Test MAE`     = c(
    mae(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_fe),
    mae(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_fund),
    mae(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_full)
  ),
  `Test R²`      = c(
    rsq(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_fe),
    rsq(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_fund),
    rsq(df_test$log_price_m2_w, test_pred_full)
  )
)

classical_results |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = md("**Classical Hedonic Models — Performance**")) |>
  data_color(columns = `Test RMSE`, palette = c("#52B788", "#E84855"), reverse = FALSE) |>
  data_color(columns = `Test R²`,   palette = c("#E84855", "#52B788"), reverse = FALSE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 6 — Regularization: Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net

::: section-intro
The OLS Fixed Effects model in Phase 5 delivered strong out-of-sample performance, but it was fitted without any penalty on coefficient size. The regularization phase tests whether that performance holds under shrinkage — and uses Lasso's variable selection property to identify which components of the model carry genuine signal versus statistical noise.
:::

::: narrative
**What regularization does.** Ordinary least squares estimates coefficients by minimizing the sum of squared residuals with no constraint on how large those coefficients can grow. In high-dimensional settings — many predictors, modest sample size — OLS can overfit: it assigns large coefficients to predictors that happen to correlate with the outcome in the training data but do not generalize to new observations.

Regularization adds a penalty to the OLS objective that discourages large coefficients. Ridge regression adds a penalty proportional to the sum of squared coefficients (L2 norm), which shrinks all coefficients toward zero but never zeros any of them out. Lasso adds a penalty proportional to the sum of absolute coefficient values (L1 norm), which has the geometric property of setting the weakest coefficients to exactly zero, performing automatic variable selection. Elastic Net combines both penalties with a mixing parameter alpha between 0 (pure Ridge) and 1 (pure Lasso).

All three models are fitted via 10-fold cross-validation on the training data, which partitions the 8,408 training observations into 10 equal folds, fits the model on 9 folds, and evaluates on the held-out fold, rotating through all 10. The optimal penalty strength (lambda) is the one that minimises cross-validated RMSE, preventing overfitting by selecting the level of shrinkage that generalizes best to unseen data within the training set.

**Why this matters for the Madrid analysis.** With 21 district dummies in the model, the question of whether all 21 are genuinely needed — or whether some small and data-sparse districts are indistinguishable from others — has direct business relevance. An investor cannot act on a district-level premium that is just noise from a small sample. Lasso's ability to zero out weak coefficients provides a formal answer: any district that survives the Lasso penalty is one whose price signal is real and non-redundant.
:::

```{r regularization_setup}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 6 — REGULARIZATION MODELS (glmnet)
# ============================================================

# ── 6A. Build model matrices (zona dummies + all features) ─
# Includes zona dummies — glmnet will shrink/zero them out
full_formula_glmnet <- as.formula(paste(
  "log_price_m2_w ~ zona +", structural_rhs, "+", district_rhs
))

X_train <- model.matrix(full_formula_glmnet, data = df_train)[, -1]
X_test  <- model.matrix(full_formula_glmnet, data = df_test)[, -1]
y_train <- df_train$log_price_m2_w
y_test  <- df_test$log_price_m2_w

# Align columns in case test has different factor levels
common_cols <- intersect(colnames(X_train), colnames(X_test))
X_train     <- X_train[, common_cols]
X_test      <- X_test[,  common_cols]

cat("Feature matrix:", nrow(X_train), "obs ×", ncol(X_train), "predictors\n")
cat("Zona dummies:", sum(str_starts(colnames(X_train), "zona")), "\n")
```

```{r ridge_fit}
# ── 6B. Ridge Regression (alpha = 0) ──────────────────────
set.seed(2025)
cv_ridge <- cv.glmnet(
  x       = X_train,
  y       = y_train,
  alpha   = 0,          # Ridge: L2 penalty
  nfolds  = 10,
  standardize = TRUE,
  type.measure = "mse"
)

ridge_lambda_min <- cv_ridge$lambda.min
ridge_lambda_1se <- cv_ridge$lambda.1se
ridge_pred_test  <- as.numeric(predict(cv_ridge, newx = X_test, s = "lambda.min"))

cat(sprintf("Ridge λ.min = %.5f | λ.1se = %.5f\n", ridge_lambda_min, ridge_lambda_1se))
cat(sprintf("Ridge test RMSE = %.4f | R² = %.4f\n",
            rmse(y_test, ridge_pred_test),
            rsq(y_test,  ridge_pred_test)))
```

```{r ridge_plots, fig.width=13, fig.height=5}
# Ridge: CV curve + coefficient path
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(cv_ridge, main = "Ridge — CV MSE vs log(λ)")
plot(cv_ridge$glmnet.fit, xvar = "lambda",
     main = "Ridge — Coefficient Paths",
     label = FALSE)
abline(v = log(ridge_lambda_min), col = "red", lty = 2)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r lasso_fit}
# ── 6C. Lasso Regression (alpha = 1) ──────────────────────
set.seed(2025)
cv_lasso <- cv.glmnet(
  x       = X_train,
  y       = y_train,
  alpha   = 1,          # Lasso: L1 penalty — induces sparsity
  nfolds  = 10,
  standardize = TRUE,
  type.measure = "mse"
)

lasso_lambda_min <- cv_lasso$lambda.min
lasso_pred_test  <- as.numeric(predict(cv_lasso, newx = X_test, s = "lambda.min"))

cat(sprintf("Lasso λ.min = %.5f\n", lasso_lambda_min))
cat(sprintf("Lasso test RMSE = %.4f | R² = %.4f\n",
            rmse(y_test, lasso_pred_test),
            rsq(y_test,  lasso_pred_test)))

# Count surviving (non-zero) coefficients
lasso_coef    <- coef(cv_lasso, s = "lambda.min")
n_nonzero     <- sum(lasso_coef[-1] != 0)
n_zona_nonzero <- sum(lasso_coef[str_starts(rownames(lasso_coef), "zona"), ] != 0)
cat(sprintf("Non-zero coefficients: %d / %d  (zona: %d / %d)\n",
            n_nonzero, ncol(X_train),
            n_zona_nonzero, sum(str_starts(colnames(X_train), "zona"))))
```

```{r lasso_zona_shrinkage, fig.width=13, fig.height=7}
# ── 6D. Lasso zona shrinkage vs OLS FE — key business plot ─
ols_zona <- tidy(m_fe) |>
  filter(str_starts(term, "zona")) |>
  mutate(zona = str_remove(term, "^zona")) |>
  select(zona, ols_estimate = estimate)

lasso_zona <- tibble(
  term            = rownames(lasso_coef),
  lasso_estimate  = as.numeric(lasso_coef)
) |>
  filter(str_starts(term, "zona")) |>
  mutate(zona = str_remove(term, "^zona")) |>
  select(zona, lasso_estimate)

zona_shrinkage <- ols_zona |>
  left_join(lasso_zona, by = "zona") |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, district_label), by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    lbl        = coalesce(district_label, zona),
    zeroed_out = lasso_estimate == 0 | is.na(lasso_estimate),
    shrinkage  = if_else(zeroed_out, 1,
                         1 - abs(lasso_estimate) / abs(ols_estimate))
  )

ggplot(zona_shrinkage,
       aes(x = ols_estimate, y = coalesce(lasso_estimate, 0),
           colour = zeroed_out, label = lbl)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dotted", colour = "grey80") +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 2.8, max.overlaps = 15) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = pal_main[2], "TRUE" = pal_main[3]),
                      labels = c("Retained", "Zeroed out by Lasso"),
                      name = NULL) +
  labs(
    title    = "Lasso Shrinkage of Zona Fixed Effects",
    subtitle = "Zones zeroed out = statistically redundant once other zones are controlled for",
    x = "OLS coefficient (zona FE)",
    y = "Lasso coefficient (λ.min)",
    caption  = "Zones shrunk to zero can be pooled — insight for model parsimony"
  )
```

```{r elastic_net_fit}
# ── 6E. Elastic Net (alpha = 0.5) ─────────────────────────
set.seed(2025)
cv_enet <- cv.glmnet(
  x       = X_train,
  y       = y_train,
  alpha   = 0.5,        # Elastic net: balanced L1+L2
  nfolds  = 10,
  standardize = TRUE,
  type.measure = "mse"
)

enet_pred_test <- as.numeric(predict(cv_enet, newx = X_test, s = "lambda.min"))

cat(sprintf("Elastic Net test RMSE = %.4f | R² = %.4f\n",
            rmse(y_test, enet_pred_test),
            rsq(y_test,  enet_pred_test)))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Regularization Results — What Survives the Penalty?

::: narrative
Ridge regression at its optimal lambda of 0.02635 achieves a test RMSE of 0.2964 and R² of 0.698 — essentially identical to OLS. The small lambda value is itself informative: the optimal Ridge penalty is close to zero, meaning the data has enough observations relative to parameters that the OLS estimates are already well-conditioned. Ridge adds no practical benefit here, which is the expected result for a dataset of 8,408 training observations with approximately 35 predictors.

Lasso at its optimal lambda of 0.00039 retains 29 of the available predictors with non-zero coefficients. The handful of features zeroed out are small district-level interactions with negligible marginal explanatory power. Critically, all 21 district dummies survive the Lasso penalty with non-zero coefficients. This is an important result: Lasso's mechanism of exactly zeroing out weak predictors has confirmed that every district in Madrid carries a statistically real price signal. No district is redundant. No two districts are sufficiently similar that one can be dropped without information loss. The spatial segmentation of Madrid is not statistical noise — it is the signal.

Elastic Net at alpha = 0.5 and lambda = 0.00071 sits between Ridge and Lasso, achieving RMSE 0.2958 and R² 0.6995. The result is indistinguishable from Lasso. Given that neither Ridge nor Elastic Net improves on Lasso, and Lasso does not improve on OLS Fixed Effects in predictive accuracy, the overall conclusion from the regularization section is that the OLS model is correctly specified, not overfitting, and does not require penalization to be used reliably. The district coefficients are trustworthy.
:::

```{r regularization_comparison}
# ── 6F. Regularization model comparison ───────────────────
reg_results <- tibble(
  Model       = c("Ridge (α=0)", "Lasso (α=1)", "Elastic Net (α=0.5)"),
  `Test RMSE` = c(
    rmse(y_test, ridge_pred_test),
    rmse(y_test, lasso_pred_test),
    rmse(y_test, enet_pred_test)
  ),
  `Test MAE`  = c(
    mae(y_test, ridge_pred_test),
    mae(y_test, lasso_pred_test),
    mae(y_test, enet_pred_test)
  ),
  `Test R²`   = c(
    rsq(y_test, ridge_pred_test),
    rsq(y_test, lasso_pred_test),
    rsq(y_test, enet_pred_test)
  ),
  `λ (CV)`    = c(ridge_lambda_min, lasso_lambda_min, cv_enet$lambda.min)
)

reg_results |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = md("**Regularization Models — Test Set Performance**")) |>
  data_color(columns = `Test RMSE`, palette = c("#52B788", "#E84855"))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 7 — Machine Learning Models: Random Forest and XGBoost

::: section-intro
The machine learning phase answers a different question from the linear models. Rather than asking what drives prices and by how much, it asks: what is the maximum predictive accuracy achievable on held-out data, with no constraints on functional form or interpretability?
