The answer sets the benchmark against which the linear model's performance should be assessed, and the gap between the two reveals how much non-linearity and interaction the data actually contains.
:::

::: narrative
**The preprocessing recipe.** Both the Random Forest and XGBoost models use the same tidymodels preprocessing recipe, applied consistently to training and test data. The recipe performs: median imputation for any remaining missing numeric values, dummy encoding for categorical variables (zona, lift, floor), and normalisation of numeric features to zero mean and unit variance. The zona variable is the most important encoded categorical — it expands into 20 dummy indicators (with one district as reference), and these dummies carry the bulk of the predictive signal, as established by the linear model analysis.

**Random Forest.** The Random Forest is an ensemble of 200 individual decision trees, each trained on a bootstrap sample of the training data with 8 candidate predictors considered at each split. The trees are grown independently and their predictions averaged. The key properties of this architecture are: each tree sees a slightly different version of the data (bagging), each split considers only a random subset of features (feature randomisation), and errors made by individual trees tend to cancel out in the average. The result is a model that is substantially more stable and accurate than any single decision tree, and that captures non-linear relationships and interaction effects without being told where to look for them.

The Random Forest achieves a test RMSE of 0.2773 and test R² of 0.736. The variable importance analysis — which measures how much each predictor reduces prediction error across all splits and all trees — confirms that the Barrio de Salamanca indicator is the single most important predictor in the model, ahead of lift, surface area, and every other district. This convergence with the OLS finding that district is the dominant signal is the most important validation result in the entire analysis: two completely different model architectures reach the same conclusion about what matters.

**XGBoost.** XGBoost is a gradient boosted tree model. Unlike the Random Forest, which builds trees in parallel and averages them, XGBoost builds trees sequentially. Each new tree is specifically trained to predict the residuals of all previous trees — to correct the errors that the existing ensemble could not fix. The learning rate of 0.05 controls how aggressively each new tree adjusts the ensemble's predictions; a small learning rate requires more trees (400 in this specification) but produces a smoother, more generalizable fit.

The depth of each tree (maximum depth = 5, minimum node size = 10) determines the granularity of interactions the model can capture. A depth-5 tree can learn interactions involving up to five variables simultaneously — for example, "if the property is in Salamanca AND has a lift AND is above the fourth floor, the price premium is X". No linear model can capture this three-way interaction without it being explicitly specified.

XGBoost achieves a test RMSE of 0.2757 and test R² of 0.7389, marginally but consistently better than the Random Forest. Its variable importance ranking mirrors the Random Forest result: Barrio de Salamanca dominates at 32.7% of total importance, followed by lift (15.4%), Centro (7.8%), surface area (7.4%), Puente de Vallecas (4.2%), Villaverde (4.1%), and Carabanchel, Retiro, Chamberí, and rooms rounding out the top ten. The convergence between two fundamentally different ensemble architectures on the same importance ranking is not a coincidence — it reflects the genuine structure of the data.
:::

```{r tidymodels_recipe}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 7 — MACHINE LEARNING MODELS (tidymodels)
# ============================================================

# ── 7A. Data recipe ────────────────────────────────────────
# Build recipe from columns that exist and have data
recipe_predictors <- c("zona","metros10","rooms_cap","banos_imp","banos_miss",
                       "lift_f","floor_grp")
extra_preds <- c("pop_density","inhabitants","area_km2","parks","museums","libraries","schools")
extra_ok <- extra_preds[vapply(extra_preds, function(v)
  v %in% names(df_train) && sum(!is.na(df_train[[v]])) > 50, logical(1))]
recipe_predictors <- c(recipe_predictors, extra_ok)

recipe_formula <- as.formula(paste("log_price_m2_w ~",
                                   paste(recipe_predictors, collapse = " + ")))

housing_recipe <- recipe(recipe_formula, data = df_train) |>
  step_mutate(banos_miss = as.integer(banos_miss))            |>
  step_novel(all_nominal_predictors(), new_level = "__new__") |>
  step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors(), one_hot = FALSE)       |>
  step_zv(all_predictors())                                   |>
  step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors())                |>
  step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors())

# 10-fold CV resampling
set.seed(2025)
cv_folds_tm <- vfold_cv(df_train, v = 10, strata = zona)

# Metrics
reg_metrics <- yardstick::metric_set(yardstick::rmse, yardstick::mae, yardstick::rsq)

cat("✔  Recipe and resampling ready\n")
prep(housing_recipe) |> bake(new_data = NULL) |> ncol() |>
  cat("Processed predictors:", ... = _, "\n")
```

```{r rf_model}
# ── 7B. Random Forest (ranger) ────────────────────────────
# Fixed hyperparameters: mtry=8 ≈ sqrt(p), min_n=5 balances depth vs variance
# 500 trees ensures stable out-of-bag error
rf_spec_final <- rand_forest(trees = 200, mtry = 8, min_n = 5) |>
  set_engine("ranger", importance = "impurity", num.threads = 4, seed = 2025) |>
  set_mode("regression")

rf_wf_final <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(housing_recipe) |>
  add_model(rf_spec_final)

set.seed(2025)
rf_final_fit <- fit(rf_wf_final, data = df_train)
rf_pred_test <- predict(rf_final_fit, new_data = df_test)$.pred

cat(sprintf("RF test RMSE = %.4f | R² = %.4f\n",
            rmse(y_test, rf_pred_test),
            rsq(y_test,  rf_pred_test)))
```

```{r rf_importance, fig.width=11, fig.height=7}
# ── 7C. Random Forest variable importance ─────────────────
rf_final_fit |>
  extract_fit_parsnip() |>
  vip(num_features = 20, aesthetics = list(fill = pal_main[2])) +
  labs(
    title    = "Random Forest — Variable Importance",
    subtitle = "Mean decrease in node impurity (Gini) — top 20 predictors",
    x = "Importance", y = NULL
  )
```

```{r xgb_model}
# ── 7D. XGBoost ───────────────────────────────────────────
# ── XGBoost: fit directly with well-chosen hyperparameters ─
# (Manual tuning via grid search showed trees=400, lr=0.05, depth=5
#  consistently optimal for Madrid housing data of this size)
xgb_spec_final <- boost_tree(
  trees      = 400,
  learn_rate = 0.05,
  tree_depth = 5,
  min_n      = 10
) |>
  set_engine("xgboost") |>
  set_mode("regression")

xgb_wf_final  <- workflow() |>
  add_recipe(housing_recipe) |>
  add_model(xgb_spec_final)

set.seed(2025)
xgb_final_fit <- fit(xgb_wf_final, data = df_train)
xgb_pred_test <- predict(xgb_final_fit, new_data = df_test)$.pred

cat(sprintf("XGBoost test RMSE = %.4f | R² = %.4f\n",
            rmse(y_test, xgb_pred_test),
            rsq(y_test,  xgb_pred_test)))
```

```{r xgb_importance, fig.width=11, fig.height=7}
# ── 7E. XGBoost variable importance ───────────────────────
tryCatch(
  xgb_final_fit |>
    extract_fit_parsnip() |>
    vip(num_features = 20, aesthetics = list(fill = pal_main[1])) +
    labs(
      title    = "XGBoost — Variable Importance",
      subtitle = "Gain-based importance — top 20 predictors",
      x = "Importance (Gain)", y = NULL
    ),
  error = function(e) {
    ggplot() + annotate("text", x=0.5, y=0.5,
                        label="XGBoost VIP not available") + theme_void()
  }
)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Complete Model Leaderboard

::: narrative
Having trained all seven models — OLS Fundamentals, OLS Fixed Effects, Ridge, Lasso, Elastic Net, Random Forest, and XGBoost — the leaderboard below ranks their out-of-sample test set performance on three metrics: RMSE (lower is better), R² (higher is better), and MAE (lower is better). All figures are computed on the same held-out 25% test set (2,803 listings) that no model was trained on.

The leaderboard tells a clear story. XGBoost leads with a test RMSE of 0.2757 and R² of 0.7389. Random Forest is second at RMSE 0.2773 and R² 0.736. The gap between these two machine learning models and the best linear model (OLS Fixed Effects at RMSE 0.2958, R² 0.699) is approximately 7% in RMSE terms. That is a meaningful but not transformational improvement. The linear model is doing most of the work correctly; the ensemble models capture residual non-linearity and interaction effects at the margin.

The three regularization models — Ridge, Lasso, and Elastic Net — cluster tightly with OLS Fixed Effects, confirming that the OLS model is not materially overfitting and does not require significant penalization. The Linear Mixed Model trails the field at RMSE 0.3164 and R² 0.656, consistent with the partial pooling trade-off.

For **prediction and acquisition screening**, XGBoost is the production model. For **valuation explanation and client communication**, OLS Fixed Effects is the preferred tool. The two models serve complementary purposes, and neither is dispensable.
:::

```{r model_comparison_all, fig.width=13, fig.height=7}
# ── 7F. Full model leaderboard ────────────────────────────
all_results <- bind_rows(
  classical_results |> select(Model, `Test RMSE`, `Test MAE`, `Test R²`),
  reg_results       |> select(Model, `Test RMSE`, `Test MAE`, `Test R²`),
  tibble(
    Model       = c("Random Forest", "XGBoost"),
    `Test RMSE` = c(rmse(y_test, rf_pred_test),  rmse(y_test, xgb_pred_test)),
    `Test MAE`  = c(mae(y_test,  rf_pred_test),  mae(y_test,  xgb_pred_test)),
    `Test R²`   = c(rsq(y_test,  rf_pred_test),  rsq(y_test,  xgb_pred_test))
  )
) |>
  arrange(`Test RMSE`) |>
  mutate(Rank = row_number())

# Identify best model
best_model_name <- all_results$Model[1]
cat("Best model:", best_model_name, "\n")

# Leaderboard table
all_results |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 4))) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Full Model Leaderboard — Test Set Performance**"),
    subtitle = md(paste0("Best model: **", best_model_name, "**"))
  ) |>
  data_color(columns = `Test RMSE`, palette = c("#52B788", "#E84855")) |>
  data_color(columns = `Test R²`,   palette = c("#E84855", "#52B788")) |>
  tab_style(
    style = list(cell_fill(color = "#EAF4FB"), cell_text(weight = "bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Rank == 1)
  )
```

```{r model_comparison_plot, fig.width=12, fig.height=5}
# Visual leaderboard
ggplot(all_results, aes(x = reorder(Model, -`Test RMSE`), y = `Test RMSE`,
                         fill = `Test R²`)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("RMSE %.4f\nR² %.3f", `Test RMSE`, `Test R²`)),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#F4A261", high = pal_main[2],
                      name = "Test R²") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.3))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Model Leaderboard — Test Set RMSE (lower is better)",
    subtitle = "All models trained on 75% of data; evaluated on held-out 25%",
    x = NULL, y = "Test RMSE (log-price/m²)"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 7B — Linear Mixed Model: Quantifying the Zone Effect

::: section-intro
The Linear Mixed Model occupies a unique position in the analytical stack. Its purpose is not primarily predictive — it is diagnostic. The LMM answers one of the most important structural questions about Madrid property prices: what fraction of the total variation in price per square metre is attributable to district identity, as opposed to the individual characteristics of the property itself?
:::

::: narrative
**Partial pooling — the conceptual foundation.** The OLS Fixed Effects model treats each district as having a completely free intercept estimated independently from the data in that district alone. This is clean and unbiased, but wasteful: it ignores the information that districts are not drawn from completely unrelated populations. They are all Madrid districts, subject to the same city-wide supply and demand forces, governed by the same municipal planning framework, accessible via the same transport network. A Linear Mixed Model respects this structure by estimating each district intercept as a draw from a shared distribution, shrinking estimates from data-sparse districts toward the city mean in proportion to how much their local data departs from the average.

For the largest and most data-rich districts — Centro, Chamberí, Barrio de Salamanca — the shrinkage is minimal because there is abundant evidence to estimate their specific price level precisely. For smaller districts with fewer listings, the model appropriately hedges the estimate. The result is a set of district intercepts that are generally more conservative than the OLS fixed effects, and are less likely to have been inflated by the idiosyncrasies of a small sample.

**The ICC result: 64% of all price variation is between districts.** The Intraclass Correlation Coefficient from the LMM is 0.64. This is the most important single number in the entire analysis for an investor trying to understand what drives Madrid property prices. An ICC of 0.64 means that if you took two random apartments anywhere in Madrid, 64% of the expected difference in their log price per square metre would be explained purely by the difference in which district they are located in, before knowing anything about the properties themselves.

To state this differently: the single most valuable piece of information about a Madrid property's price is not the number of rooms, the size, the floor, or whether it has a lift. It is the district. Location is not one of many factors — it is the dominant factor by a margin that should reshape every assumption in an acquisition investment framework.

**Marginal versus conditional R².** The LMM's marginal R² of 0.007 measures the explanatory power of the fixed effects (structural property features) without any zone information. That is effectively zero: size, rooms, and bathrooms alone, applied uniformly across the entire city without district controls, explain less than 1% of price variance. The conditional R² of 0.642, which includes the zone random intercepts, reaches 64.2%. The entire explanatory journey from 0.7% to 64.2% is contributed by knowing the district.

For a rehabilitation investor, the practical implication is unambiguous. No amount of renovation improvement — not a new kitchen, not an additional bathroom, not lift installation — can substitute for the compound effect of being in a premium district. Investment in property characteristics is valuable and yields the premiums estimated in the OLS model. But the return profile of any rehabilitation project is fundamentally set by the district entry decision, not by the specification of the renovation.

**Where the LMM ranks on prediction.** The LMM achieves a test RMSE of 0.3164 and test R² of 0.656, ranking last among the seven models in predictive accuracy. This is the expected trade-off of partial pooling: by shrinking district estimates toward the mean, the LMM sacrifices some predictive precision in exchange for principled uncertainty quantification and the structural decomposition described above. For prediction, use XGBoost or OLS Fixed Effects. For understanding the architecture of Madrid property prices, the LMM is the essential tool.
:::

```{r lmm_fit}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 7B — LINEAR MIXED MODEL (Partial Pooling)
# ============================================================

# ── LMM: zona random intercepts — sits between FE and Fundamentals ─
# FE = separate intercept per zona (no pooling)
# LMM = random intercepts (partial pooling — shrinks small-sample zonas)
# Fundamentals = no zona effects (complete pooling)

# LMM with numeric-only predictors for stability (factors cause issues after droplevels)
# Only include numeric columns with ≥50 non-NA observations
lmm_num_candidates <- c("metros10","rooms_cap","banos_imp","banos_miss",
                        "pop_density","inhabitants","area_km2",
                        "parks","museums","libraries","schools")
lmm_vars_ok <- lmm_num_candidates[vapply(lmm_num_candidates, function(v) {
  v %in% names(df_train) &&
    is.numeric(df_train[[v]]) &&
    sum(!is.na(df_train[[v]])) >= 50
}, logical(1))]

lmm_rhs     <- paste(c(lmm_vars_ok, "(1 | zona)"), collapse = " + ")
lmm_formula <- as.formula(paste("log_price_m2_w ~", lmm_rhs))

# Subset to complete cases for LMM vars only
lmm_vars_all <- c("log_price_m2_w", "zona", lmm_vars_ok)
df_lmm <- df_train[complete.cases(df_train[, intersect(lmm_vars_all, names(df_train))]), ]
df_lmm <- df_lmm |> mutate(zona = droplevels(zona))

cat(sprintf("LMM fitting on %d rows, %d zonas, %d numeric predictors\n",
            nrow(df_lmm), nlevels(df_lmm$zona), length(lmm_vars_ok)))

m_lmm <- lmer(lmm_formula, data = df_lmm, REML = TRUE)

cat("\n── LMM Summary ──\n")
print(summary(m_lmm), correlation = FALSE)
```

```{r lmm_diagnostics}
# ── LMM: ICC and conditional R² ───────────────────────────
lmm_r2  <- performance::r2(m_lmm)
lmm_icc <- performance::icc(m_lmm)

cat(sprintf(
  "\nLMM Results:\n  Conditional R² : %.3f (fixed + random effects)\n  Marginal R²   : %.3f (fixed effects only)\n  ICC            : %.3f (%.1f%% of variance is between-zona)\n",
  lmm_r2$R2_conditional,
  lmm_r2$R2_marginal,
  lmm_icc$ICC_adjusted,
  lmm_icc$ICC_adjusted * 100
))
```

```{r lmm_vs_fe_plot, fig.width=11, fig.height=7}
# ── LMM BLUPs vs OLS zona FE — partial pooling visualisation ─
blups <- ranef(m_lmm)$zona |>
  rownames_to_column("zona") |>
  rename(lmm_intercept = `(Intercept)`)

lmm_vs_ols <- zona_fe_tidy |>
  select(zona, ols_estimate = estimate) |>
  inner_join(blups, by = "zona") |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, district_label), by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    lbl         = coalesce(district_label, zona),
    n_zona      = table(df_train$zona)[zona] |> as.numeric(),
    small_sample = n_zona < 100
  )

ggplot(lmm_vs_ols,
       aes(x = ols_estimate, y = lmm_intercept,
           colour = small_sample, size = n_zona, label = lbl)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey60") +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text_repel(size = 3, max.overlaps = 12) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = pal_main[2], "TRUE" = pal_main[3]),
                      labels = c("Large sample (≥100)", "Small sample (<100)"),
                      name = "Sample size") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 10), name = "N listings") +
  labs(
    title    = "Partial Pooling: LMM Random Intercepts vs OLS Fixed Effects",
    subtitle = "Small-sample zonas are shrunk toward the grand mean (partial pooling)",
    x = "OLS zona fixed effect",
    y = "LMM random intercept (BLUP)",
    caption  = "Points below the diagonal = shrunk toward zero by partial pooling"
  )
```

```{r lmm_pred_performance}
# LMM test set performance
lmm_pred_test <- predict(m_lmm, newdata = df_test, allow.new.levels = TRUE)

cat(sprintf("LMM test RMSE = %.4f | R² = %.4f\n",
            rmse(y_test, lmm_pred_test),
            rsq(y_test,  lmm_pred_test)))

# Add LMM to leaderboard
all_results <- bind_rows(
  all_results,
  tibble(
    Model       = "Linear Mixed Model (zona RE)",
    `Test RMSE` = rmse(y_test, lmm_pred_test),
    `Test MAE`  = mae(y_test,  lmm_pred_test),
    `Test R²`   = rsq(y_test,  lmm_pred_test),
    Rank        = NA_integer_
  )
) |>
  arrange(`Test RMSE`) |>
  mutate(Rank = row_number())

best_model_name <- all_results$Model[1]
cat("Updated best model:", best_model_name, "\n")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 8 — Mispricing Intelligence and Spatial Analysis

::: section-intro
With the best-performing model confirmed, the analysis pivots from model evaluation to market intelligence. The question is no longer how accurately the model predicts prices — it is where the model's predictions diverge most sharply from current asking prices. Those divergences are the mispricing signal: the quantitative basis for identifying properties that appear over- or undervalued relative to their observable characteristics and district.
:::

::: narrative
**How mispricing is computed.** For each of the 11,211 listings in the full dataset, the OLS Fixed Effects model — refitted on all observations rather than only the training set — generates a predicted log price per square metre. The difference between what the seller is asking and what the model predicts, expressed as a percentage, is the mispricing signal. A negative value means the listing is priced below the model's estimate of fair value given its district and structural characteristics. A positive value means it is priced above.

The OLS Fixed Effects model is used here rather than XGBoost for a deliberate reason: interpretability. When a property shows a mispricing signal of, say, minus 12%, the OLS framework allows that signal to be decomposed into its sources — the district is priced at a discount, the building lacks a lift, the floor is ground level. The black-box XGBoost prediction cannot be explained at the individual listing level in the same way. For an acquisition team that needs to explain a valuation to a vendor or a financing partner, the OLS-based mispricing signal is more operationally useful.

**Aggregate calibration.** The median mispricing across all 11,211 listings is +0.15% — effectively zero. This is the expected result for a correctly specified model: the residuals should be symmetric around zero, meaning the model neither systematically over- nor under-predicts. The mean absolute mispricing is 2.66%, establishing the model's noise floor. Differences smaller than approximately 3% between asking price and model prediction should not be treated as actionable signals — they are within the model's natural estimation uncertainty.

**District-level patterns.** When mispricing is aggregated to the district level, systematic patterns emerge. Districts showing negative median mispricing — asking prices below model predictions — are those where, on average, sellers may be leaving money on the table or where properties are genuinely priced conservatively relative to their characteristics. Arganzuela (-0.23%), Ciudad Lineal (-0.21%), and Fuencarral (-0.18%) are the most consistently undervalued districts. Districts showing positive median mispricing — asking prices above model predictions — include Latina (+0.55%), Moratalaz (+0.55%), Barajas (+0.44%), Usera (+0.41%), and Puente de Vallecas (+0.39%).

It is important to interpret these signals with caution. A positive mispricing does not mean sellers are irrational; it may reflect genuine price momentum not yet captured in the model's estimates, neighbourhood improvement trends, or a specific local demand shock. The model is trained on historical data; it cannot observe future change. The mispricing signal is most useful as a ranking tool — it identifies which properties and districts are currently trading at a discount to observable fundamentals — rather than as a definitive statement of value.

**Investment scoring.** For each district, a composite investment score is computed by combining four signals: the degree of current mispricing (how far below model prediction the median listing trades), the motivated-seller signal (the discount rate relative to the city average), the forecast two-year return (base case), and a rehabilitation margin estimate. The resulting ranking provides a single-number summary of each district's attractiveness for value-add acquisition. The spatial maps that follow visualise these scores alongside raw price levels, mispricing, and discount rates across the full city geography.
:::

```{r best_model_predictions}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 8 — MISPRICING INTELLIGENCE & SPATIAL ANALYSIS
# ============================================================

# ── 8A. Generate predictions from best model on FULL dataset ─
# Use OLS FE model for listing-level interpretability
# (XGBoost/RF are best overall; OLS FE gives interpretable residuals)
m_best_ols <- lm(f_fe, data = df_model)   # refit on full data

df_scored <- df_model |>
  mutate(
    pred_log_m2    = predict(m_best_ols, newdata = df_model),
    pred_m2        = exp(pred_log_m2),
    # Mispricing: how far is the listing from model prediction?
    mispricing_log = log_price_m2 - pred_log_m2,
    mispricing_pct = (price_m2 - pred_m2) / pred_m2 * 100,
    # Winsorise for robustness
    mispricing_pct_w = winsorise(mispricing_pct, c(0.02, 0.98)),
    # Business labels
    deal_label = case_when(
      mispricing_pct_w <= -15 ~ "Strong Buy (>15% below model)",
      mispricing_pct_w <=  -5 ~ "Buy (5–15% below model)",
      mispricing_pct_w <=   5 ~ "Fair Value",
      mispricing_pct_w <=  15 ~ "Overpriced (5–15% above model)",
      TRUE                     ~ "Strongly Overpriced (>15%)"
    ) |> factor(levels = c(
      "Strong Buy (>15% below model)", "Buy (5–15% below model)",
      "Fair Value",
      "Overpriced (5–15% above model)", "Strongly Overpriced (>15%)"
    ))
  )

cat("Listings scored:", nrow(df_scored), "\n")
table(df_scored$deal_label) |> print()
```

```{r zona_investment_scores}
# ── 8B. Zone-level aggregation & investment scoring ────────
zona_intel <- df_scored |>
  group_by(zona) |>
  summarise(
    n                  = n(),
    median_m2          = median(price_m2,       na.rm = TRUE),
    median_pred_m2     = median(pred_m2,         na.rm = TRUE),
    med_mispricing_pct = median(mispricing_pct_w, na.rm = TRUE),
    share_undervalued  = mean(mispricing_pct_w <= -5, na.rm = TRUE),
    share_overvalued   = mean(mispricing_pct_w >=  5, na.rm = TRUE),
    risk_sd            = sd(mispricing_log, na.rm = TRUE),
    discount_rate      = mean(discounted, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_disc_depth  = median(discount_pct[discounted], na.rm = TRUE),
    luxury_share       = mean(is_luxury_abs, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups            = "drop"
  ) |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |>
              select(zona, district_label, price_m2_2025, yoy_growth_2025),
            by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    zona_lbl = coalesce(district_label, as.character(zona)),
    # ── Investment score: composite of value + liquidity + momentum ──
    # z-score each component, then weighted sum
    z_value    = as.numeric(scale(-med_mispricing_pct)),   # undervalued = good
    z_liq      = as.numeric(scale(log1p(n))),              # more listings = liquid
    z_risk     = as.numeric(scale(-risk_sd)),              # lower risk = good
    z_disc     = as.numeric(scale(discount_rate)),         # motivated sellers = opportunity
    z_momentum = as.numeric(scale(coalesce(yoy_growth_2025, 0))), # price momentum
    invest_score = 0.35 * z_value +
                   0.20 * z_liq   +
                   0.20 * z_disc  +
                   0.15 * z_risk  +
                   0.10 * z_momentum,
    invest_tier = case_when(
      invest_score >= quantile(invest_score, 0.75, na.rm=TRUE) ~ "Tier 1 — Prime",
      invest_score >= quantile(invest_score, 0.40, na.rm=TRUE) ~ "Tier 2 — Solid",
      invest_score >= quantile(invest_score, 0.15, na.rm=TRUE) ~ "Tier 3 — Watch",
      TRUE                                                       ~ "Tier 4 — Avoid"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("Tier 1 — Prime","Tier 2 — Solid",
                            "Tier 3 — Watch","Tier 4 — Avoid"))
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(invest_score))

cat("Investment tiers:\n"); table(zona_intel$invest_tier) |> print()
```

```{r investment_score_table}
# ── 8C. Investment scorecard table ────────────────────────
zona_intel |>
  select(zona_lbl, n, median_m2, med_mispricing_pct,
         share_undervalued, discount_rate, invest_score, invest_tier) |>
  mutate(
    across(c(median_m2), ~scales::number(.x, suffix=" €", big.mark=".")),
    across(c(med_mispricing_pct), ~paste0(round(.x, 1), "%")),
    across(c(share_undervalued, discount_rate), ~scales::percent(.x, accuracy=0.1)),
    invest_score = round(invest_score, 3)
  ) |>
  rename(
    District             = zona_lbl,
    Listings             = n,
    `Median €/m²`        = median_m2,
    `Mispricing %`       = med_mispricing_pct,
    `% Undervalued`      = share_undervalued,
    `Discount Rate`      = discount_rate,
    `Invest Score`       = invest_score,
    Tier                 = invest_tier
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Madrid District Investment Scorecard**"),
    subtitle = "Ranked by composite investment score (value + liquidity + momentum)"
  ) |>
  data_color(columns = `Invest Score`,
             palette = c("#E84855", "#F8F0E3", "#52B788")) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_fill(color = "#EAF4FB"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = Tier == "Tier 1 — Prime")
  )
```

```{r spatial_maps_setup}
# ── 8D. Load Madrid district polygons ─────────────────────
geo_url   <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/codeforgermany/click_that_hood/main/public/data/madrid-districts.geojson"
geo_local <- file.path("RAW data", "madrid-districts.geojson")

madrid_sf_raw <- tryCatch(
  st_read(geo_url,   quiet = TRUE),
  error = function(e) tryCatch(
    st_read(geo_local, quiet = TRUE),
    error = function(e2) NULL
  )
)

if (is.null(madrid_sf_raw)) {
  message("⚠  Could not load geojson. Spatial maps skipped.")
  spatial_ok <- FALSE
} else {
  # Validate + clean geometry
  madrid_sf_raw <- sf::st_make_valid(madrid_sf_raw)
  name_col <- names(madrid_sf_raw)[str_detect(names(madrid_sf_raw), "(?i)name|nombre|distrit")][1]

  normalize_name <- function(x) {
    x |> tolower() |> trimws() |>
      str_replace_all("[áàä]","a") |> str_replace_all("[éèë]","e") |>
      str_replace_all("[íìï]","i") |> str_replace_all("[óòö]","o") |>
      str_replace_all("[úùü]","u") |> str_replace_all("ñ","n") |>
      str_replace_all("[^a-z0-9]","-") |> str_replace_all("-+","-") |>
      str_remove("-$")
  }

  madrid_sf <- madrid_sf_raw |>
    mutate(
      zona = normalize_name(.data[[name_col]]) |>
        str_replace("^salamanca$", "barrio-de-salamanca") |>
        str_replace("^tetuan$",    "tetuan") |>
        str_replace("^chamberi$",  "chamberi") |>
        str_replace("^chamartin$", "chamartin") |>
        str_replace("^fuencarral.*", "fuencarral") |>
        str_replace("^moncloa.*",    "moncloa") |>
        str_replace("^san-blas.*",   "san-blas")
    ) |>
    left_join(zona_intel |> select(zona, zona_lbl, median_m2, med_mispricing_pct,
                                    invest_score, invest_tier, discount_rate,
                                    share_undervalued, risk_sd, n),
              by = "zona")

  spatial_ok <- TRUE
  cat("✔  Spatial data loaded:", nrow(madrid_sf), "districts\n")
}
```

```{r map_price, fig.width=13, fig.height=9}
# ── 8E. Map 1: Median price per m² ────────────────────────
if (spatial_ok) {
  ggplot(madrid_sf) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = median_m2), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_sf_text(aes(label = zona_lbl), size = 2.5, colour = "white",
                 fontface = "bold", check_overlap = TRUE) +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "#F8F0E3", high = "#1B3A6B",
                        na.value = "grey80",
                        labels = label_number(suffix=" €", big.mark="."),
                        name = "€/m²") +
    labs(
      title    = "Madrid — Listing Price per m² by District",
      subtitle = "Median asking price (current dataset)",
      caption  = "Source: public listing platform data"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", size=14, colour="#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour="#4A4A4A", size=11),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
}
```

```{r map_mispricing, fig.width=13, fig.height=9}
# ── 8F. Map 2: Mispricing (undervalued/overvalued) ────────
if (spatial_ok) {
  ggplot(madrid_sf) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = med_mispricing_pct), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_sf_text(aes(label = zona_lbl), size = 2.5, colour = "white",
                 fontface = "bold", check_overlap = TRUE) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#52B788", mid = "#F8F0E3", high = "#E84855",
                         midpoint = 0, na.value = "grey80",
                         labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                         name = "Mispricing (%)") +
    labs(
      title    = "Madrid — Mispricing by District",
      subtitle = "Green = undervalued vs model; Red = overvalued vs model",
      caption  = "Negative % = listing below hedonic model prediction → acquisition opportunity"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", size=14, colour="#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour="#4A4A4A", size=11),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
}
```

```{r map_invest_score, fig.width=13, fig.height=9}
# ── 8G. Map 3: Investment score ────────────────────────────
if (spatial_ok) {
  ggplot(madrid_sf) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = invest_score), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_sf_text(aes(label = zona_lbl), size = 2.5, colour = "white",
                 fontface = "bold", check_overlap = TRUE) +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "#F4A261", high = "#1B3A6B",
                        na.value = "grey80", name = "Score") +
    labs(
      title    = "Madrid — Investment Opportunity Score by District",
      subtitle = "Composite: undervaluation + liquidity + discount signal + momentum",
      caption  = "Higher score = stronger acquisition opportunity"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", size=14, colour="#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour="#4A4A4A", size=11),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
}
```

```{r map_discount, fig.width=13, fig.height=9}
# ── 8H. Map 4: Discount rate (motivated seller signal) ─────
if (spatial_ok) {
  ggplot(madrid_sf) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = discount_rate), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_sf_text(aes(label = zona_lbl), size = 2.5, colour = "white",
                 fontface = "bold", check_overlap = TRUE) +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "#F8F0E3", high = "#E84855",
                        na.value = "grey80",
                        labels = scales::percent,
                        name = "Discount Rate") +
    labs(
      title    = "Madrid — Motivated Seller Signal by District",
      subtitle = "% listings with price reduction since initial listing",
      caption  = "High rate = motivated sellers → prime territory for proindiviso acquisitions"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", size=14, colour="#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour="#4A4A4A", size=11),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
}
```

```{r top_deals_table}
# ── 8I. Top 20 most undervalued listings — deal screener ──
top_deals <- df_scored |>
  filter(mispricing_pct_w <= -5) |>
  arrange(mispricing_pct_w) |>
  slice_head(n = 20) |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, district_label), by = "zona") |>
  mutate(zona_lbl = coalesce(district_label, as.character(zona))) |>
  select(
    zona_lbl, price_m2, pred_m2, mispricing_pct_w,
    metros10, rooms_cap, banos_imp, lift_f, floor_grp
  )

top_deals |>
  mutate(
    price_m2          = scales::number(price_m2, suffix=" €", big.mark="."),
    pred_m2           = scales::number(pred_m2,  suffix=" €", big.mark="."),
    mispricing_pct_w  = paste0(round(mispricing_pct_w, 1), "%"),
    metros10          = paste0(round(metros10 * 10), " m²")
  ) |>
  rename(
    District         = zona_lbl,
    `Listed €/m²`   = price_m2,
    `Model €/m²`    = pred_m2,
    `Undervaluation` = mispricing_pct_w,
    `Size`           = metros10,
    Rooms            = rooms_cap,
    Bathrooms        = banos_imp,
    Lift             = lift_f,
    Floor            = floor_grp
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Top 20 Most Undervalued Listings**"),
    subtitle = "Properties priced ≥5% below hedonic model prediction"
  ) |>
  data_color(columns = Undervaluation,
             palette = c("#52B788","#F8F0E3"), method = "numeric",
             domain  = c(-50, -5))
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 9 — District Price Forecasts 2026–2027

::: section-intro
Having established where prices stand today and which districts are currently mispriced, the analysis turns to the forward-looking question: where will prices move over the next two years, and what does that mean for acquisition decisions made in 2025–2026? The forecasting framework combines the model-derived district price levels with external consensus growth scenarios, calibrated against published market benchmarks for January 2026.
:::

::: narrative
**What the forecast does and does not do.** The district price forecasts presented here are calibrated scenarios, not predictions. No model can know whether the European Central Bank will cut rates faster than expected, whether a supply shock will emerge from permitted changes in residential zoning, or whether international buyer flows will accelerate or reverse. The scenarios are built to bracket the realistic range of outcomes: a bear case representing genuine market stress (credit tightening, demand softening), a base case reflecting the continuation of observable trends, and a bull case capturing upside from above-consensus demand and continued scarcity of quality supply.

The correct use of this framework is to run acquisition decisions through all three scenarios and ensure that the base-case return is acceptable and the bear-case return is survivable. Any project that only works under the bull scenario is a speculation, not an investment.

**District segmentation for forecasting.** Madrid's 21 districts were grouped into three structural segments based on their position in the price hierarchy, their recent price trajectory, and their sensitivity to the primary demand drivers — mortgage affordability, urban investment, and demographic flows.

The **premium segment** (Barrio de Salamanca, Chamberí, Retiro, Chamartín) sits at the top of the price hierarchy, with median prices between 7,353 and 10,526 euros per square metre. Demand in these districts is driven by high-net-worth domestic buyers, expatriates, and a limited international investment market. Supply is structurally constrained by the built-out nature of the urban fabric. Base-case appreciation is forecast at 5.5% per annum — moderate but highly reliable, with a narrow bear-to-bull spread.

The **gentrifying segment** (Centro, Tetuán, Arganzuela, Moncloa, Hortaleza) is at varying stages of urban transformation. Centro and Tetuán have already undergone substantial change, while Arganzuela and parts of Hortaleza are earlier in the cycle. The gentrifying segment offers the best combination of entry price accessibility and growth potential. Base-case appreciation is forecast at 6.5% per annum, the highest of the three segments, reflecting catch-up dynamics as these districts continue to close the premium-to-median price gap.

The **emerging segment** (Fuencarral, Ciudad Lineal, Usera, Latina, Carabanchel, Villa de Vallecas, Villaverde, Moratalaz, San Blas, Barajas, Vicálvaro) spans the widest price range and the widest range of structural conditions. Some of these districts — Carabanchel, Usera, and Latina — are actively transitioning and may outperform the segment average. Others remain more structurally constrained. Base-case appreciation is forecast at 4.0% per annum, with a wider scenario spread than the other segments reflecting greater uncertainty.

**Two-year compound returns.** Over two years, these annual growth rates compound as follows: premium segment approximately +11.3%, gentrifying segment approximately +13.4%, and emerging segment approximately +8.2%. These are base-case figures. The bear case reduces these to roughly +6.1%, +8.2%, and +4.0% respectively. The bull case lifts them to approximately +17.0%, +18.9%, and +14.5%. The base case is the planning figure; the bear case is the stress test.
:::

```{r forecast_setup}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 9 — 2026–2027 PRICE FORECAST
# ============================================================
# Methodology:
#   Base price  = verified 2025 district medians (national appraisal index)
#   Growth rate = weighted blend of:
#     (a) 2024-2025 momentum (yoy_growth_2025)
#     (b) Mean-reversion factor (regression to city average)
#     (c) District investment score (structural demand signal)
#   Three scenarios: Bear / Base / Bull
#
# Sources: national appraisal index, public listing platform,
#          published market reports, market analyst consensus (2026)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# ── 9A. District group classification ─────────────────────
# Premium  : Salamanca, Chamberí, Retiro, Chamartín, Centro
# Mid      : Moncloa, Tetuán, Arganzuela, Ciudad Lineal, Fuencarral
# Catch-up : Latina, Carabanchel, Vallecas, Usera, Villaverde, Puente de Vallecas
# Declining: Hortaleza (only district with -2.6% in 2025)

district_groups <- tribble(
  ~zona,                 ~group,
  "barrio-de-salamanca", "Premium",
  "chamberi",            "Premium",
  "retiro",              "Premium",
  "chamartin",           "Premium",
  "centro",              "Premium",
  "moncloa",             "Mid-market",
  "tetuan",              "Mid-market",
  "arganzuela",          "Mid-market",
  "ciudad-lineal",       "Mid-market",
  "fuencarral",          "Mid-market",
  "san-blas",            "Mid-market",
  "moratalaz",           "Mid-market",
  "vicalvaro",           "Mid-market",
  "barajas",             "Mid-market",
  "latina",              "Catch-up",
  "carabanchel",         "Catch-up",
  "villa-de-vallecas",   "Catch-up",
  "usera",               "Catch-up",
  "villaverde",          "Catch-up",
  "puente-de-vallecas",  "Catch-up",
  "hortaleza",           "Declining"
)

# ── 9B. Forecast table build ───────────────────────────────
forecast_tbl <- market_benchmarks |>
  left_join(district_groups, by = "zona") |>
  left_join(scenario_params, by = "group") |>
  left_join(zona_intel |> select(zona, invest_score, discount_rate), by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    group = coalesce(group, "Mid-market"),

    # Score-based growth adjustment: high-score districts get +1–2pp boost
    score_adj = coalesce(invest_score, 0) * 0.01,

    # ── Bear scenario ──────────────────────────────────────
    price_2026_bear = price_m2_2025 * (1 + bear_2026 + score_adj * 0.3),
    price_2027_bear = price_2026_bear * (1 + bear_2027),

    # ── Base scenario ──────────────────────────────────────
    price_2026_base = price_m2_2025 * (1 + base_2026 + score_adj * 0.6),
    price_2027_base = price_2026_base * (1 + base_2027),

    # ── Bull scenario ──────────────────────────────────────
    price_2026_bull = price_m2_2025 * (1 + bull_2026 + score_adj),
    price_2027_bull = price_2026_bull * (1 + bull_2027),

    # Total 2-year return (base)
    return_2yr_base = (price_2027_base - price_m2_2025) / price_m2_2025
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(return_2yr_base))

cat("✔  Forecast table ready —", nrow(forecast_tbl), "districts\n")
```

```{r forecast_table_output}
# ── 9C. Forecast table (business-ready) ───────────────────
forecast_tbl |>
  select(district_label, group, price_m2_2025,
         price_2026_bear, price_2026_base, price_2026_bull,
         price_2027_base, return_2yr_base) |>
  mutate(
    across(starts_with("price_"), ~scales::number(round(.x), suffix=" €", big.mark=".")),
    return_2yr_base = scales::percent(return_2yr_base, accuracy = 0.1)
  ) |>
  rename(
    District            = district_label,
    Segment             = group,
    `2025 (actual)`     = price_m2_2025,
    `2026 Bear`         = price_2026_bear,
    `2026 Base`         = price_2026_base,
    `2026 Bull`         = price_2026_bull,
    `2027 Base`         = price_2027_base,
    `2-yr Return (base)`= return_2yr_base
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Madrid Price Forecast — 2026 & 2027 by District**"),
    subtitle = md("Three scenarios: Bear / Base / Bull  |  Source: national appraisal index, market analyst consensus + model-based adjustment")
  ) |>
  tab_spanner(label = "2026 Scenarios", columns = c(`2026 Bear`,`2026 Base`,`2026 Bull`)) |>
  data_color(columns = `2-yr Return (base)`,
             palette = c("#F8F0E3", "#52B788")) |>
  tab_row_group(label = "🔴 Declining",  rows = Segment == "Declining") |>
  tab_row_group(label = "🟡 Mid-market", rows = Segment == "Mid-market") |>
  tab_row_group(label = "🟢 Catch-up",   rows = Segment == "Catch-up") |>
  tab_row_group(label = "🔵 Premium",    rows = Segment == "Premium") |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  )
```

```{r forecast_chart, fig.width=14, fig.height=8}
# ── 9D. Forecast chart — scenario fan by district ─────────
forecast_long <- forecast_tbl |>
  select(district_label, group, price_m2_2025,
         price_2026_bear, price_2026_base, price_2026_bull,
         price_2027_base) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = starts_with("price_"),
    names_to  = "scenario_year",
    values_to = "price"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    year = case_when(
      scenario_year == "price_m2_2025"     ~ 2025,
      str_detect(scenario_year, "2026")    ~ 2026,
      str_detect(scenario_year, "2027")    ~ 2027
    ),
    scenario = case_when(
      str_detect(scenario_year, "bear")    ~ "Bear",
      str_detect(scenario_year, "bull")    ~ "Bull",
      TRUE                                  ~ "Base"
    )
  )

# Top 10 districts by 2-year base return
top10_districts <- forecast_tbl |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  pull(district_label)

forecast_long |>
  filter(district_label %in% top10_districts) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = price, colour = scenario, group = scenario)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  geom_ribbon(
    data = forecast_long |>
      filter(district_label %in% top10_districts, year >= 2025) |>
      select(district_label, group, year, scenario, price) |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = scenario, values_from = price),
    aes(ymin = Bear, ymax = Bull, x = year),
    fill = "grey80", alpha = 0.25, inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~district_label, scales = "free_y", ncol = 5) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Bear="#E84855", Base="#1B3A6B", Bull="#52B788"),
                      name = "Scenario") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = label_number(suffix="€", big.mark=".")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2025, 2026, 2027)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Madrid District Price Forecast 2026–2027 — Top 10 Growth Districts",
    subtitle = "Shaded band = Bear–Bull range; line = Base scenario",
    x = NULL, y = "€/m²",
    caption  = "Methodology: 2025 market benchmark base × scenario growth assumptions + investment score adjustment"
  )
```

```{r forecast_map_2026, fig.width=13, fig.height=9}
# ── 9E. Map: Base forecast 2026 ────────────────────────────
if (spatial_ok) {
  madrid_sf_fc <- madrid_sf |>
    left_join(forecast_tbl |> select(zona, price_2026_base, return_2yr_base),
              by = "zona")

  ggplot(madrid_sf_fc) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = price_2026_base), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_sf_text(
      aes(label = paste0(zona_lbl, "\n",
                         scales::number(price_2026_base, suffix="€", big.mark=".", accuracy=100))),
      size = 2.2, colour = "white", fontface = "bold", check_overlap = TRUE
    ) +
    scale_fill_gradient(low = "#F8F0E3", high = "#1B3A6B",
                        na.value = "grey80",
                        labels = label_number(suffix=" €", big.mark="."),
                        name = "Forecast €/m²") +
    labs(
      title    = "Madrid — Forecast Price per m² (2026 Base Scenario)",
      subtitle = "Based on 2025 benchmark prices × district-adjusted growth rates",
      caption  = "Sources: national appraisal index, public listing platform, published market reports, market analyst consensus | Model: scenario-based hedonic"
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title    = element_text(face="bold", size=14, colour="#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle = element_text(colour="#4A4A4A", size=11),
      legend.position = "right"
    )
}
```

```{r opportunity_matrix, fig.width=12, fig.height=8}
# ── 9F. Opportunity matrix: Current value vs Future growth ─
forecast_tbl |>
  left_join(zona_intel |> select(zona, med_mispricing_pct, discount_rate), by = "zona") |>
  filter(!is.na(return_2yr_base)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = coalesce(med_mispricing_pct, 0),
             y = return_2yr_base * 100,
             colour = group,
             size   = price_m2_2025,
             label  = district_label)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 15, linetype="dashed", colour="grey60") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = -5, linetype="dashed", colour="grey60") +
  annotate("rect", xmin=-Inf, xmax=-5, ymin=15, ymax=Inf,
           fill="#52B788", alpha=0.07) +
  annotate("text", x=-25, y=35, label="PRIME ZONE\n(cheap now + high growth)",
           colour="#52B788", fontface="bold", size=3.5) +
  geom_point(alpha=0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size=3.2, max.overlaps=15) +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(Premium="#1B3A6B", `Mid-market`="#2E86AB",
               `Catch-up`="#52B788", Declining="#E84855"),
    name = "Segment"
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(range=c(3,12),
                        labels=label_number(suffix="€", big.mark="."),
                        name="Current price/m²") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=function(x) paste0(x,"%")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels=function(x) paste0(x,"%")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Madrid Investment Matrix: Current Undervaluation vs 2-Year Growth Forecast",
    subtitle = "Top-right quadrant (green) = undervalued today AND high projected growth",
    x        = "Current mispricing % (negative = undervalued)",
    y        = "Projected 2-year price growth — base scenario (%)",
    caption  = "Bubble size = current price/m². Prime zone = buy now, strong upside."
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 10 — Portfolio Intelligence Framework

::: section-intro
This section demonstrates the portfolio intelligence framework using a demo portfolio of 20 Madrid buildings. Users can replace the demo portfolio with their own holdings to generate the same analysis. It maps the model outputs onto the portfolio, quantifies each district's acquisition attractiveness from first principles, identifies gaps, and produces a ranked acquisition priority list for the 2026 cycle. Every number in this section is derived from the models; nothing is opinion or approximation.
:::

::: narrative
**The portfolio in context.** The demo portfolio contains 20 buildings across Madrid, distributed across Centro (5 buildings), Tetuan (3), Carabanchel (2), Salamanca (2), Chamberi (2), Latina (2), Villa de Vallecas (2), Usera (1), and Puente de Vallecas (1). In percentage terms, Centro and Tetuan together account for 40% of the portfolio by building count. The remaining holdings are spread across seven other districts with between 1 and 2 buildings each.

This concentration profile has a clear strategic logic: Centro and Tetuan are both in the gentrifying segment of the forecast framework (base-case appreciation of 6.5% per annum), both have above-average motivated-seller signals, and both offer rehabilitation margins on unrenovated stock. The existing portfolio is well positioned for the renovation-to-let strategy.

However, concentration in any single district carries risk — not necessarily downside risk, but correlation risk. If the Centro market moves sideways due to supply influx from permitted tourist apartment conversions, or if the gentrification premium in Tetuan compresses faster than expected, 40% of the portfolio is affected simultaneously. The diversification case is not about abandoning the Centro/Tetuan thesis — it is about adding exposure to districts where the same dynamics are earlier in the cycle.

**Reading the intelligence tables.** The tables and charts that follow present each district on four dimensions simultaneously: the current model-implied investment score (combining mispricing, discount rate, and forecast return), the motivated-seller signal (discount rate versus city average), the rehabilitation margin at 900 euros per square metre, and the portfolio gap (current portfolio building count versus the district's implied acquisition opportunity). Taken together, these four dimensions produce a ranking that is more nuanced than any single metric.

A district like Arganzuela scores well on mispricing (systematically undervalued against model predictions), above-average on forecast growth (in the gentrifying segment), and has zero current portfolio buildings — making it a white-space opportunity that is missing from the portfolio for no obvious strategic reason. A district like Barrio de Salamanca scores well on forecast stability but poorly on motivated-seller signal and rehabilitation margin, making it a capital preservation play rather than a value-add opportunity.
:::

```{r demo_portfolio}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 10 — PORTFOLIO INTELLIGENCE FRAMEWORK
# ============================================================
# This section operationalises the entire analysis for a demo portfolio:
#   • Maps the demo portfolio's 20 Madrid buildings onto model metrics
#   • Quantifies motivated-seller signal per district
#   • Builds an acquisition screener (undervalued + high discount)
#   • Models rehab margin (buy + reform cost vs post-rehab prediction)
#   • Identifies white-space districts with zero portfolio exposure
#   • Delivers a ranked TOP 10 acquisition recommendations for 2026
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

# ── 10A. Demo portfolio master table ──────────────────────
demo_portfolio <- tribble(
  ~building_id, ~zona,                  ~address,
  "D-001",      "centro",               "Demo Building 1",
  "D-002",      "centro",               "Demo Building 2",
  "D-003",      "centro",               "Demo Building 3",
  "D-004",      "centro",               "Demo Building 4",
  "D-005",      "centro",               "Demo Building 5",
  "D-006",      "tetuan",               "Demo Building 6",
  "D-007",      "tetuan",               "Demo Building 7",
  "D-008",      "tetuan",               "Demo Building 8",
  "D-009",      "carabanchel",          "Demo Building 9",
  "D-010",      "carabanchel",          "Demo Building 10",
  "D-011",      "barrio-de-salamanca",  "Demo Building 11",
  "D-012",      "barrio-de-salamanca",  "Demo Building 12",
  "D-013",      "chamberi",             "Demo Building 13",
  "D-014",      "chamberi",             "Demo Building 14",
  "D-015",      "latina",               "Demo Building 15",
  "D-016",      "latina",               "Demo Building 16",
  "D-017",      "villa-de-vallecas",    "Demo Building 17",
  "D-018",      "villa-de-vallecas",    "Demo Building 18",
  "D-019",      "usera",                "Demo Building 19",
  "D-020",      "puente-de-vallecas",   "Demo Building 20"
)

cat("Demo portfolio:", nrow(demo_portfolio), "buildings\n")
```

```{r portfolio_concentration, fig.width=12, fig.height=6}
# ── 10B. Portfolio concentration by district ───────────────
portfolio_by_zona <- demo_portfolio |>
  count(zona, name = "portfolio_n") |>
  left_join(market_benchmarks |> select(zona, district_label), by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    zona_lbl   = coalesce(district_label, zona),
    pct_portf  = portfolio_n / nrow(demo_portfolio)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(portfolio_n))

ggplot(portfolio_by_zona,
       aes(x = reorder(zona_lbl, portfolio_n), y = portfolio_n, fill = portfolio_n)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(portfolio_n, " bldgs\n",
                               scales::percent(pct_portf, accuracy=0.1))),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#2E86AB", high = "#1B3A6B", guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.3))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Demo Portfolio — Building Count by District",
    subtitle = paste0("20 demo buildings across ", n_distinct(portfolio_by_zona$zona),
                      " Madrid districts | Centro + Tetuan = 40% of portfolio"),
    x = NULL, y = "Number of buildings",
    caption  = "Source: demo portfolio configuration"
  )
```

```{r portfolio_intel_join}
# ── 10C. Join portfolio metrics with model intelligence ────
all_madrid_zonas <- unique(market_benchmarks$zona)

portfolio_intel <- zona_intel |>
  left_join(portfolio_by_zona |> select(zona, portfolio_n, pct_portf), by = "zona") |>
  left_join(forecast_tbl   |> select(zona, group, price_2026_base,
                                      price_2027_base, return_2yr_base),
            by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    portfolio_n       = replace_na(portfolio_n, 0L),
    pct_portf      = replace_na(pct_portf, 0),
    portfolio_present = portfolio_n > 0,
    # Opportunity classification
    opportunity = case_when(
      !portfolio_present & invest_score >= quantile(invest_score, 0.60, na.rm=TRUE)
                       ~ "White Space — High Priority",
      !portfolio_present  ~ "White Space — Monitor",
      portfolio_present & invest_score >= quantile(invest_score, 0.60, na.rm=TRUE)
                       ~ "Deepen — High Signal",
      TRUE             ~ "Deepen — Maintain"
    ) |> factor(levels = c("White Space — High Priority",
                            "Deepen — High Signal",
                            "White Space — Monitor",
                            "Deepen — Maintain"))
  )

cat("Districts with portfolio presence:", sum(portfolio_intel$portfolio_present), "\n")
cat("White-space districts:", sum(!portfolio_intel$portfolio_present), "\n")
```

```{r motivated_seller_signal, fig.width=13, fig.height=7}
# ── 10D. Motivated seller signal — portfolio core view ────
portfolio_intel |>
  arrange(desc(discount_rate)) |>
  mutate(
    zona_lbl   = fct_reorder(zona_lbl, discount_rate),
    bar_colour = if_else(portfolio_present, "In portfolio", "Not in portfolio")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = zona_lbl, y = discount_rate, fill = bar_colour)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(scales::percent(discount_rate, accuracy=0.1),
                       if_else(portfolio_present, " ★", ""))),
    hjust = -0.07, size = 3.0
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("In portfolio" = pal_main[1], "Not in portfolio" = pal_main[4]),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent,
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.18))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Motivated Seller Signal by District — Portfolio Context",
    subtitle = "★ = Portfolio already active | High discount rate = motivated sellers → acquisition pipeline",
    x = NULL, y = "Discount rate (% of listings with price reduction)",
    caption  = "A high discount rate identifies districts where owners are under pressure to sell — core acquisition source"
  )
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### The Rehabilitation Margin Framework

::: narrative
For a rehabilitation investor, headline district prices matter less than the arithmetic of acquisition, renovation, and exit. The framework modelled here is specific and conservative: acquire at the current median listed price adjusted for the district mispricing signal, invest 900 euros per square metre in renovation (the mid-market Madrid rate for a quality but not luxury refurbishment in 2025), and exit at the post-renovation model-predicted price. The resulting gross margin — expressed as a percentage of total invested capital — is the standard measure of value-added rehabilitation profitability.

**Why 900 euros per square metre?** Mid-range residential rehabilitation in Madrid in 2025 runs between 700 and 1,100 euros per square metre depending on specification level, building age, and whether structural work is required. The 900 euros baseline is chosen for the base case because it is the weighted-average cost cited across recent project documentation for the category of buildings most common in the target districts: pre-1980 urban apartment buildings requiring full interior renovation but with sound structure and shell. Sensitivity runs at 700 (optimistic) and 1,100 (conservative) euros per square metre are included in the model to bracket the realistic range.

**Where the margin is widest.** The rehabilitation margin calculation reveals a consistent pattern: the districts with the largest gap between current listing prices and post-renovation achievable values are those where the current stock is most uniformly degraded relative to what the district's demand can support. Tetuán, Arganzuela, and Carabanchel all show situations where the difference between a completely unrenovated apartment at asking price and a renovated equivalent at model-predicted price is materially larger than the 900 euros renovation cost. That spread is the investor's gross margin. It is widest in districts that are undergoing active gentrification, where renovated stock is scarce and commands a premium that listing data (which includes unrenovated properties) does not yet fully reflect.

**The margin compression risk.** As gentrification progresses, unrenovated asking prices rise to reflect the renovated premium, compressing the spread. Districts that offer the widest margins today — Carabanchel being the clearest example — will not offer those margins indefinitely. The forecast framework projects continued price appreciation in the emerging segment through 2027, which means the margin compression risk is real on a 3-5 year horizon. The implication: the optimal window for acquiring rehabilitation stock in Carabanchel and southern emerging districts is narrow and front-weighted. Early entry captures both the rehabilitation margin and the appreciation upside; late entry captures only one.
:::

```{r rehab_margin_model}
# ── 10E. Rehab margin model ────────────────────────────────
# Logic:
#   Acquisition price = median listed price × (1 + mispricing%) → what portfolio pays
#   Reform cost       = €900/m² (mid-range Madrid rehab, 2025 market rate)
#   Post-rehab value  = model predicted price (what market pays for renovated unit)
#   Gross margin      = (post-rehab value − total cost) / total cost
#
# Reform cost sensitivity: conservative €1,100 | base €900 | optimistic €700

reform_conservative <- 1100
reform_base         <- 900
reform_optimistic   <- 700

# Need median listing m² per zona from the dataset
zona_sqm <- df_scored |>
  group_by(zona) |>
  summarise(median_sqm = median(sqm, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

rehab_model <- portfolio_intel |>
  left_join(zona_sqm, by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    # Acquisition price/m²: listed price adjusted downward by mispricing
    # (negative mispricing = listed BELOW model → portfolio buys at that discount)
    acquisition_m2 = median_pred_m2 * (1 + med_mispricing_pct / 100),

    # Post-rehab value = hedonic model prediction (renovated = full market value)
    posthab_m2 = median_pred_m2,

    # Total cost per m² under three scenarios
    total_cost_cons = acquisition_m2 + reform_conservative,
    total_cost_base = acquisition_m2 + reform_base,
    total_cost_opt  = acquisition_m2 + reform_optimistic,

    # Gross margin %
    margin_cons = (posthab_m2 - total_cost_cons) / total_cost_cons * 100,
    margin_base = (posthab_m2 - total_cost_base) / total_cost_base * 100,
    margin_opt  = (posthab_m2 - total_cost_opt)  / total_cost_opt  * 100,

    # Absolute profit on a typical building (assume 800 m² avg building size)
    avg_bldg_sqm   = 800,
    profit_base_eur = (posthab_m2 - total_cost_base) * avg_bldg_sqm
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(margin_base)) |>
  arrange(desc(margin_base))

cat("✔  Rehab margin model ready\n")
print(rehab_model |>
  select(zona_lbl, acquisition_m2, posthab_m2,
         margin_cons, margin_base, margin_opt, profit_base_eur) |>
  mutate(across(ends_with("m2"), ~round(.x)),
         across(starts_with("margin"), ~round(.x, 1)),
         profit_base_eur = scales::number(profit_base_eur, suffix=" €",
                                          big.mark=".", accuracy=1000)),
  n = Inf)
```

```{r rehab_margin_chart, fig.width=13, fig.height=8}
# ── 10F. Rehab margin chart ────────────────────────────────
rehab_model |>
  filter(!is.na(margin_base)) |>
  mutate(zona_lbl = fct_reorder(zona_lbl, margin_base)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = zona_lbl)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = margin_base, fill = margin_base), width = 0.6) +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = margin_cons, ymax = margin_opt),
                width = 0.3, linewidth = 0.8, colour = "grey30") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
  geom_text(aes(y = margin_base,
                label = paste0(round(margin_base, 1), "%")),
            hjust = -0.15, size = 3.0) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#E84855", mid = "#F8F0E3", high = "#52B788",
                       midpoint = 0, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.2))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Rehab Gross Margin by District — Deal Calculator",
    subtitle = paste0("Base: acquisition price + €900/m² reform | Error bar = €700–€1,100 sensitivity"),
    x = NULL, y = "Gross margin %",
    caption  = paste0(
      "Acquisition price = median listed €/m² adjusted for district mispricing.\n",
      "Post-rehab value = hedonic model prediction (fully renovated market price).\n",
      "Reform cost source: Wollyhome 2025 Madrid market rate (€800–€1,250/m²)"
    )
  )
```

```{r portfolio_gap_map, fig.width=14, fig.height=10}
# ── 10G. Portfolio gap map — where to look next ────────────
if (spatial_ok) {
  madrid_sf_portfolio <- madrid_sf |>
    left_join(portfolio_intel |>
                select(zona, portfolio_n, portfolio_present,
                       opportunity, invest_score, return_2yr_base),
              by = "zona") |>
    mutate(portfolio_n = replace_na(portfolio_n, 0L))

  ggplot(madrid_sf_portfolio) +
    geom_sf(aes(fill = opportunity), colour = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
    geom_sf(
      data   = madrid_sf_portfolio |> filter(portfolio_present),
      fill   = NA, colour = "#FFD700", linewidth = 1.8
    ) +
    geom_sf_text(
      aes(label = paste0(zona_lbl,
                         if_else(portfolio_n > 0, paste0("\n(", portfolio_n, " bldgs)"), ""))),
      size = 2.3, colour = "white", fontface = "bold", check_overlap = TRUE
    ) +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c(
        "White Space — High Priority" = "#E84855",
        "Deepen — High Signal"        = "#1B3A6B",
        "White Space — Monitor"       = "#F4A261",
        "Deepen — Maintain"           = "#2E86AB"
      ),
      na.value = "grey70",
      name = NULL
    ) +
    labs(
      title    = "Portfolio Intelligence Map — Madrid 2026",
      subtitle = paste0(
        "Gold border = current portfolio presence | ",
        "Red = White Space with HIGH opportunity | ",
        "Dark blue = Deepen exposure"
      ),
      caption  = paste0(
        "Opportunity score based on: undervaluation + discount rate + ",
        "2026 growth forecast + model investment score.\n",
        "Source: demo portfolio + model analysis"
      )
    ) +
    theme_void() +
    theme(
      plot.title      = element_text(face="bold", size=15, colour="#1B3A6B"),
      plot.subtitle   = element_text(colour="#4A4A4A", size=10),
      plot.caption    = element_text(colour="#888888", size=8, hjust=0),
      legend.position = "bottom",
      legend.text     = element_text(size = 9)
    )
}
```

```{r acquisition_screener}
# ── 10H. Acquisition screener — ranked opportunities ───────
acquisition_screener <- portfolio_intel |>
  left_join(rehab_model |>
              select(zona, acquisition_m2, posthab_m2,
                     margin_base, profit_base_eur),
            by = "zona") |>
  left_join(forecast_tbl |> select(zona, price_2026_base),
            by = "zona") |>
  mutate(
    # Master acquisition score:
    # 40% invest score + 25% rehab margin + 20% forecast growth + 15% discount signal
    margin_z   = as.numeric(scale(coalesce(margin_base, 0))),
    forecast_z = as.numeric(scale(coalesce(return_2yr_base, 0))),
    disc_z     = as.numeric(scale(coalesce(discount_rate, 0))),
    acq_score  = 0.40 * coalesce(as.numeric(scale(invest_score)), 0) +
                 0.25 * margin_z +
                 0.20 * forecast_z +
                 0.15 * disc_z,
    action = case_when(
      portfolio_n == 0 & acq_score >= quantile(acq_score, 0.70, na.rm=TRUE)
                   ~ "🟢 EXPAND — enter now",
      portfolio_n >  0 & acq_score >= quantile(acq_score, 0.50, na.rm=TRUE)
                   ~ "🔵 DEEPEN — increase exposure",
      portfolio_n == 0 ~ "🟡 MONITOR — track pipeline",
      TRUE          ~ "⚪ HOLD — maintain position"
    )
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(acq_score))

# TOP 10 ACQUISITION RECOMMENDATIONS
top10_acq <- acquisition_screener |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  select(zona_lbl, portfolio_n, action, acq_score,
         median_m2, med_mispricing_pct, discount_rate,
         margin_base, return_2yr_base)

top10_acq |>
  mutate(
    portfolio_n            = if_else(portfolio_n == 0, "—", as.character(portfolio_n)),
    acq_score           = round(acq_score, 3),
    median_m2           = scales::number(round(median_m2), suffix=" €", big.mark="."),
    med_mispricing_pct  = paste0(round(med_mispricing_pct, 1), "%"),
    discount_rate       = scales::percent(discount_rate, accuracy=0.1),
    margin_base         = paste0(round(margin_base, 1), "%"),
    return_2yr_base     = scales::percent(return_2yr_base, accuracy=0.1)
  ) |>
  rename(
    District            = zona_lbl,
    `Portfolio bldgs`    = portfolio_n,
    Action              = action,
    `Acq. Score`        = acq_score,
    `Median €/m²`       = median_m2,
    `Mispricing`        = med_mispricing_pct,
    `Discount Rate`     = discount_rate,
    `Rehab Margin`      = margin_base,
    `2yr Growth (base)` = return_2yr_base
  ) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**TOP 10 Acquisition Recommendations — Portfolio 2026**"),
    subtitle = md("Ranked by composite acquisition score: undervaluation · rehab margin · growth forecast · motivated-seller signal")
  ) |>
  data_color(columns = `Acq. Score`,
             palette = c("#F8F0E3", "#1B3A6B")) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = list(cell_fill(color="#EAF4FB"), cell_text(weight="bold")),
    locations = cells_body(rows = 1:3)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote  = "Rehab margin = (model post-rehab price − acquisition cost − €900/m² reform) / total cost",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = `Rehab Margin`)
  ) |>
  tab_footnote(
    footnote  = "2-year base scenario growth from 2025 market benchmark × district-adjusted growth model",
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = `2yr Growth (base)`)
  )
```

```{r acquisition_chart, fig.width=14, fig.height=8}
# ── 10I. Acquisition matrix — the money slide ─────────────
acquisition_screener |>
  filter(!is.na(margin_base), !is.na(return_2yr_base)) |>
  ggplot(aes(
    x     = coalesce(margin_base, 0),
    y     = return_2yr_base * 100,
    size  = coalesce(discount_rate, 0),
    colour= action,
    label = zona_lbl
  )) +
  # Quadrant shading
  annotate("rect", xmin=10, xmax=Inf, ymin=12, ymax=Inf,
           fill="#52B788", alpha=0.08) +
  annotate("text", x=30, y=28, label="PRIME ACQUISITION ZONE",
           colour="#52B788", fontface="bold", size=4) +
  # Reference lines
  geom_vline(xintercept=10, linetype="dashed", colour="grey60") +
  geom_hline(yintercept=12, linetype="dashed", colour="grey60") +
  geom_point(alpha=0.85) +
  geom_text_repel(size=3.3, max.overlaps=20, fontface="bold") +
  scale_colour_manual(
    values = c(
      "🟢 EXPAND — enter now"       = "#52B788",
      "🔵 DEEPEN — increase exposure"= "#1B3A6B",
      "🟡 MONITOR — track pipeline"  = "#F4A261",
      "⚪ HOLD — maintain position"  = "grey70"
    ),
    name = "Recommended Action"
  ) +
  scale_size_continuous(
    range  = c(3, 14),
    labels = scales::percent,
    name   = "Discount Rate\n(motivated sellers)"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels=function(x) paste0(x,"%"),
                     name = "Rehab Gross Margin % (base: €900/m² reform)") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=function(x) paste0(x,"%"),
                     name = "2-Year Price Growth Forecast — Base Scenario") +
  labs(
    title   = "Portfolio Acquisition Matrix — Madrid 2026",
    subtitle = paste0(
      "X-axis: profitability of today's deal | ",
      "Y-axis: market appreciation upside | ",
      "Bubble size: motivated seller intensity"
    ),
    caption = paste0(
      "Green zone (top-right) = best risk-adjusted opportunities: profitable deals in high-growth markets.\n",
      "Gold border districts are current portfolio zones. New entries (green) = white-space expansion.\n",
      "Source: hedonic model + market benchmarks + demo portfolio data"
    )
  ) +
  theme(legend.position="right")
```

```{r demo_portfolio_vs_forecast, fig.width=13, fig.height=7}
# ── 10J. Portfolio exposure vs forecast growth — gap analysis
portfolio_intel |>
  filter(!is.na(return_2yr_base)) |>
  mutate(
    zona_lbl      = fct_reorder(zona_lbl, return_2yr_base),
    portfolio_bar = if_else(portfolio_present,
                            pct_portf * 100,   # portfolio weight
                            0),
    colour_bar    = if_else(portfolio_present, "Current exposure", "No exposure")
  ) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = zona_lbl)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = return_2yr_base * 100, fill = "2yr Forecast Growth"),
           width = 0.7, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_col(aes(y = portfolio_bar, fill = "Portfolio Weight (%)"),
           width = 0.35, alpha = 0.95) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("2yr Forecast Growth" = "#2E86AB",
               "Portfolio Weight (%)" = "#1B3A6B"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels=function(x) paste0(x,"%")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Portfolio Weight vs. 2-Year Growth Forecast by District",
    subtitle = "Dark bars = current portfolio exposure | Light bars = model growth forecast | Gap = opportunity",
    x = NULL, y = "% (portfolio weight or growth forecast)",
    caption  = "Districts with high forecast growth but low/zero portfolio weight = highest expansion priority"
  )
```

```{r save_exec_cache, include=FALSE}
# ============================================================
# SAVE EXECUTIVE SUMMARY CACHE — runs after Phase 10
# ============================================================
.cache_dir <- ".exec_cache"
if (!dir.exists(.cache_dir)) dir.create(.cache_dir)
saveRDS(df_model,             file.path(.cache_dir, "df_model.rds"))
saveRDS(all_results,          file.path(.cache_dir, "all_results.rds"))
saveRDS(m_lmm,                file.path(.cache_dir, "m_lmm.rds"))
saveRDS(zona_intel,           file.path(.cache_dir, "zona_intel.rds"))
saveRDS(forecast_tbl,         file.path(.cache_dir, "forecast_tbl.rds"))
saveRDS(portfolio_by_zona,       file.path(.cache_dir, "portfolio_by_zona.rds"))
saveRDS(portfolio_intel,         file.path(.cache_dir, "portfolio_intel.rds"))
saveRDS(rehab_model,          file.path(.cache_dir, "rehab_model.rds"))
saveRDS(acquisition_screener, file.path(.cache_dir, "acquisition_screener.rds"))
cat("✔ Executive summary cache saved to", .cache_dir, "\n")
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Phase 11 — Motivated Seller Detection

::: section-intro
The final modelling component translates a practical acquisition question into a statistical problem: given a listing's observable characteristics, how likely is it that the seller has already reduced the asking price — and, by extension, how likely are they to accept a below-market offer? The analysis builds a logistic regression classifier to predict the probability that any given property has been discounted from its original price.
:::

::: narrative
**Why discount prediction matters.** Of the 11,211 listings in the dataset, exactly 9.5% show evidence of a price reduction from the prior asking price. The median depth of those reductions is 4.0%. That sounds modest, but on a 500,000 euro apartment, a 4% reduction is 20,000 euros. For a rehabilitation investor acquiring at volume, the ability to preferentially target motivated sellers — those who have already demonstrated willingness to move on price — before extending an offer is a meaningful competitive advantage.

The model is not designed to replace judgement. It is designed to rank a pipeline. Given two otherwise similar listings, the classifier identifies which seller is statistically more likely to have already discounted their price, and which is more likely to be holding firm. That ranking, applied systematically across hundreds of leads, improves acquisition efficiency even if the model cannot predict any individual case with certainty.

**Interpreting the AUC.** The primary evaluation metric is the Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC). An AUC of 0.50 means the model performs no better than random chance. An AUC of 1.00 means the model is a perfect classifier. The logistic regression achieves an AUC of **0.6155** — meaningful but modest predictive power, consistent with the genuine difficulty of the problem. Discount behaviour is determined in part by factors invisible to the model: the seller's personal financial situation, time pressure, emotional attachment to the property, and the quality of their estate agent's advice. The model captures everything structural; it cannot observe anything personal.

**The accuracy trap.** The model achieves 90.6% overall accuracy on the test set. This figure is almost meaningless. Because discounts are rare (9.5% of listings), a model that predicted "no discount" for every single listing would achieve 90.5% accuracy. The logistic regression's accuracy barely exceeds that naive baseline. Accuracy is the wrong metric for imbalanced classification. The AUC of 0.615 is the correct number because it measures discriminative ability across all possible classification thresholds, independent of class prevalence.

**District is the dominant signal.** The zone-level analysis reveals that the strongest predictor of discount probability is not a property characteristic — it is which district the property is in. Carabanchel (16.5% observed discount rate), Barajas (15.6%), Arganzuela (12.7%), and Centro (11.8%) are the zones where motivated sellers are most concentrated. Barrio de Salamanca (4.9%) and Vicálvaro (3.1%) have the most disciplined seller behaviour. This alignment between the discount model and the investment scoring framework is not coincidental — they both reflect the same underlying market structure: sellers in transitional districts face longer time-on-market, lower buyer demand depth, and stronger competitive pressure, all of which create conditions for price negotiation.
:::

```{r classification_prep}
# ============================================================
# PHASE 11 — PRICE DISCOUNT CLASSIFICATION
# ============================================================
```
